narrative:
"Enough of this. And what, then, is to be regarded as the origin of government? Will not a man be able to judge of it best from a point of view in which he may behold the progress of states and their transitions to good or evil?"

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that he might watch them from the point of view of time, and observe the changes which take place in them during infinite ages."

"How so?"

"Why, do you think that you can reckon the time which has elapsed since cities first existed and men were citizens of them?"

"Hardly."

"But are sure that it must be vast and incalculable?"

"Certainly."

"And have not thousands and thousands of cities come into being during this period and as many perished? And has not each of them had every form of government many times over, now growing larger, now smaller, and again improving or declining?"

"To be sure."

"Let us endeavour to ascertain the cause of these changes; for that will probably explain the first origin and development of forms of government."

"Very good. You shall endeavour to impart your thoughts to us, and we will make an effort to understand you."

"Do you believe that there is any truth in ancient traditions?"

"What traditions?"

"The traditions about the many destructions of mankind which have been occasioned by deluges and pestilences, and in many other ways, and of the survival of a remnant?"

"Every one is disposed to believe them."

"Let us consider one of them, that which was caused by the famous deluge."

"What are we to observe about it?"

"I mean to say that those who then escaped would only be hill shepherds -- small sparks of the human race preserved on the tops of mountains."

"Clearly."

"Such survivors would necessarily be unacquainted with the arts and the various devices which are suggested to the dwellers in cities by interest or ambition, and with all the wrongs which they contrive against one another."

"Very true."

"Let us suppose, then, that the cities in the plain and on the sea-coast were utterly destroyed at that time."

"Very good."

"Would not all implements have then perished and every other excellent invention of political or any other sort of wisdom have utterly disappeared?"
refashioned:
"Let us set that aside. What should we consider the origin of government? Surely the best way to understand it is to observe the progress of states over time -- how they shift between good and evil conditions."

"What do you mean by that?"

"I mean we should study them across the sweep of time, watching the changes that occur over countless ages."

"How would we do that?"

"Well, can you calculate how long it has been since cities first existed and people became citizens?"

"That would be almost impossible."

"But you are certain it must be an enormously long time?"

"Absolutely."

"And during that time, haven't thousands upon thousands of cities come into existence and just as many been destroyed? Hasn't every one of them cycled through every form of government multiple times -- expanding, contracting, flourishing, and declining?"

"Without question."

"Then let us try to determine what causes these changes, because that will likely reveal the original source and development of political systems."

"An excellent plan. Share your thinking with us, and we will do our best to follow."

"Do you think there is truth in ancient traditions?"

"Which traditions do you mean?"

"The ones about the many destructions of the human race -- caused by floods, plagues, and various other catastrophes -- where only a remnant survives."

"Everyone is inclined to believe such accounts."

"Let us focus on one particular case: the great deluge."

"What should we consider about it?"

"Those who escaped would have been nothing more than mountain shepherds -- tiny sparks of humanity preserved on the highest peaks."

"Obviously."

"These survivors would have known nothing of the arts, technologies, and clever schemes that city life breeds through self-interest and ambition, including all the ways people devise to wrong one another."

"That is certainly true."

"So let us assume that every city on the plains and along the coastlines was completely destroyed."

"Agreed."

"In that case, wouldn't all tools have been lost, and every sophisticated invention of political or practical wisdom have vanished entirely?"
elegant:
What is the origin of government? The best vantage point is the long view of time -- watching states rise and fall across countless ages.

Thousands of cities have come into existence and perished, each cycling through every form of government. To understand why these changes happen is to discover the origin of political systems themselves.

Ancient traditions tell of repeated catastrophes -- floods, plagues -- that wiped out civilization, leaving only mountain shepherds as survivors. These remnants knew nothing of the arts, technologies, or political schemes of city life.

If the coastal and lowland cities were completely destroyed, then all tools, inventions, and political wisdom would have vanished with them.
summary: The Athenian proposes studying the origin of government by examining the rise and fall of states across vast stretches of time. He introduces the idea that ancient catastrophes like the great deluge periodically destroy civilization, leaving only isolated mountain shepherds who lack all knowledge of arts, technology, and political life.
distilled: Government's origins can be traced by examining how civilizations cyclically rise from catastrophic destruction, with only primitive mountain survivors remaining to rebuild.
notes: Speaker: The Athenian Stranger leads the inquiry; Cleinias responds. -- This passage opens Book 3's central investigation into the origins of political society. The cyclical catastrophe theory echoes Timaeus 22a-23c (the Egyptian priest's account of periodic destructions) and Critias 109d-110a. The 'hill shepherds' as 'small sparks' of humanity anticipates the state-of-nature reconstructions in Rousseau and Hobbes. The method of studying government through historical observation parallels the approach in the Statesman (268d-274e), where the Eleatic Stranger uses myth to frame political evolution. The deluge tradition connects to Near Eastern flood narratives known to the Greeks.

narrative: "Why, yes, my friend; and if things had always continued as they are at present ordered, how could any discovery have ever been made even in the least particular? For it is evident that the arts were unknown during ten thousand times ten thousand years. And no more than a thousand or two thousand years have elapsed since the discoveries of Daedalus, Orpheus and Palamedes -- since Marsyas and Olympus invented music, and Amphion the lyre -- not to speak of numberless other inventions which are but of yesterday."
refashioned: "Exactly, my friend. And consider this: if the world had always remained in its current state of development, how could anything ever have been discovered in the first place? The arts were clearly unknown for an almost infinite stretch of time -- countless millions of years. It has been only a thousand or two thousand years since the discoveries attributed to Daedalus, Orpheus, and Palamedes -- since Marsyas and Olympus created music, and Amphion invented the lyre. And there are countless other inventions that are practically from yesterday."
elegant: If civilization had always existed in its current form, nothing would ever have needed inventing. The arts were unknown for immeasurable ages. The great discoveries attributed to Daedalus, Orpheus, Palamedes, and the inventors of music are only a thousand or two thousand years old -- and countless other inventions are practically from yesterday.
summary: Cleinias agrees that civilization is recent, noting that the arts were unknown for vast stretches of time. He points out that famous inventions attributed to figures like Daedalus, Orpheus, and the creators of music are only one or two thousand years old, with many other discoveries being even more recent.
distilled: The arts and inventions of civilization are astonishingly recent compared to the immense span of human existence.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- Cleinias demonstrates genuine philosophical engagement, not merely agreeing but extending the argument. The figures named represent different domains of civilization: Daedalus (craftsmanship, engineering), Orpheus (poetry, theology), Palamedes (writing, mathematics, strategy), Marsyas and Olympus (wind instruments), Amphion (stringed instruments). The vast timescales echo Timaeus 23d-e. The idea that civilization is a thin, recent layer atop immense ages of ignorance anticipates modern archaeological perspectives on the brevity of recorded history.

narrative:
"Have you forgotten, Cleinias, the name of a friend who is really of yesterday?"

"I suppose that you mean Epimenides."

"The same, my friend; he does indeed far overleap the heads of all mankind by his invention; for he carried out in practice, as you declare, what of old Hesiod only preached."

"Yes, according to our tradition."

"After the great destruction, may we not suppose that the state of man was something of this sort: In the beginning of things there was a fearful illimitable desert and a vast expanse of land; a herd or two of oxen would be the only survivors of the animal world; and there might be a few goats, these too hardly enough to maintain the shepherds who tended them?"

"True."

"And of cities or governments or legislation, about which we are now talking, do you suppose that they could have any recollection at all?"

"None whatever."

"And out of this state of things has there not sprung all that we now are and have: cities and governments, and arts and laws, and a great deal of vice and a great deal of virtue?"

"What do you mean?"

"Why, my good friend, how can we possibly suppose that those who knew nothing of all the good and evil of cities could have attained their full development, whether of virtue or of vice?"

"I understand your meaning, and you are quite right."

"But, as time advanced and the race multiplied, the world came to be what the world is."

"Very true."

"Doubtless the change was not made all in a moment, but little by little, during a very long period of time."

"A highly probable supposition."

"At first, they would have a natural fear ringing in their ears which would prevent their descending from the heights into the plain."

"Of course."
refashioned:
"Have you forgotten, Cleinias, a certain friend of ours who is practically a contemporary?"

"You must mean Epimenides."

"Exactly. He truly surpassed everyone with his achievement, for he put into practice what Hesiod had only preached in theory."

"So our tradition holds."

"Now, after the great destruction, we can imagine the condition of humanity was something like this: a vast, terrifying, empty landscape stretching in every direction. Perhaps a herd or two of cattle survived, and maybe a few goats -- barely enough to sustain the handful of shepherds who tended them."

"That seems right."

"And as for cities, governments, or laws -- the very things we are discussing now -- would these survivors have had any memory of them at all?"

"Absolutely none."

"Yet from this state of utter desolation has sprung everything we now possess: our cities, governments, arts, laws -- along with an abundance of both vice and virtue."

"What exactly do you mean?"

"Think about it: how could people who knew nothing of the good and evil that come with city life have developed the full range of human character, whether virtuous or vicious?"

"I see your point, and you are entirely correct."

"As time passed and the human race grew in numbers, the world gradually became what it is today."

"Indeed."

"And this transformation surely did not happen overnight, but unfolded slowly over an enormously long period."

"That is a highly reasonable assumption."

"At first, the memory of the catastrophe would have kept them fearful, preventing them from leaving the mountain heights for the plains below."

"Naturally."
elegant:
The Athenian mentions Epimenides -- a recent Cretan who put Hesiod's teachings into practice -- then reconstructs the post-catastrophe world: a vast empty landscape with barely enough livestock to sustain a few shepherds, with no memory of cities, governments, or laws.

From this desolation sprang everything we now have -- politics, arts, virtue, and vice alike. But people who had never experienced city life could not have developed the full range of moral character. Civilization emerged gradually, over immense stretches of time. The survivors' fear of the catastrophe initially kept them confined to the mountain heights.
summary: After a brief mention of Epimenides as a recent Cretan innovator, the Athenian reconstructs post-catastrophe humanity: a tiny population of shepherds with no memory of civilization. He argues that both virtue and vice develop only through the experience of communal life, and that the rebuilding of civilization was an extremely gradual process driven partly by lingering fear of disaster.
distilled: Civilization and moral complexity emerged gradually from a post-catastrophe state of primitive simplicity, as fear slowly gave way to expansion from mountain refuges.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger leads; Cleinias responds. -- Epimenides of Crete (c. 7th-6th century BCE) was a legendary sage, purifier, and prophet associated with Crete. His mention flatters Cleinias's homeland. The reference to Hesiod connects to Works and Days, particularly the advice about moderation and right living. The idea that virtue and vice require the conditions of social life to develop is a key Platonic theme -- compare Republic 369b-372d (the 'healthy city' that has no need for luxuries or the guardian class). The gradual emergence of civilization parallels the myth of Prometheus in Protagoras 320d-322d. The psychological detail of fear keeping survivors on heights shows Plato's interest in how emotion shapes political development.

narrative:
"The fewness of the survivors at that time would have made them all the more desirous of seeing one another; but then the means of travelling either by land or sea had been almost entirely lost, as I may say, with the loss of the arts, and there was great difficulty in getting at one another; for iron and brass and all metals were jumbled together and had disappeared in the chaos; nor was there any possibility of extracting ore from them; and they had scarcely any means of felling timber. Even if you suppose that some implements might have been preserved in the mountains, they must quickly have worn out and vanished, and there would be no more of them until the art of metallurgy had again revived."

"There could not have been."

"In how many generations would this be attained?"

"Clearly, not for many generations."

"During this period, and for some time afterwards, all the arts which require iron and brass and the like would disappear."

"Certainly."

"Faction and war would also have died out in those days, and for many reasons."

"How would that be?"
refashioned:
"The small number of survivors would have made them eager to find one another, but travel by land or sea had become nearly impossible with the loss of practical knowledge. There was enormous difficulty in reaching other people. Iron, bronze, and all metals had been mixed together and buried in the destruction -- there was no way to extract ore. They had almost no means of cutting down trees. Even if a few tools had survived in the mountains, they would have quickly worn out and disappeared, with no replacements possible until the art of metalworking was rediscovered."

"That must be so."

"How many generations would it take to recover that knowledge?"

"Clearly, a great many."

"And during all that time, every craft that depends on iron, bronze, or similar metals would vanish."

"Without doubt."

"Political conflict and war would also have disappeared during this period, for several reasons."

"How so?"
elegant:
The survivors would have longed for human contact, but with the loss of the arts, travel became nearly impossible. Metals were lost in the chaos, ore could not be extracted, and timber could not be felled. Any surviving tools would quickly wear out with no replacements until metallurgy was reinvented -- a process requiring many generations.

During this long period without metal tools, all dependent crafts would vanish. Political faction and war would also disappear.
summary: The Athenian explains that while the few survivors would have craved contact with one another, the loss of technology made travel nearly impossible. Metals were lost, tools could not be replaced, and it would take many generations to rediscover metallurgy. Without metal-based technology, both the practical arts and organized warfare would have disappeared.
distilled: The loss of metallurgy after catastrophe made travel, craftsmanship, and organized warfare impossible for many generations.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with brief responses from an interlocutor. -- This passage offers a remarkably materialist analysis for Plato, grounding political development in technological capacity. The dependency chain -- metallurgy enables tools, tools enable crafts, crafts enable complex society -- anticipates modern archaeological periodization (Stone Age, Bronze Age, Iron Age). The observation that war requires technology connects to the Republic's analysis of the 'luxurious city' (372d-373e) where warfare arises from excess and expansion. The detail about metals being 'jumbled together' in chaos reflects ancient knowledge of how floods and geological upheaval could destroy mining operations and smelt works.

narrative:
"In the first place, the desolation of these primitive men would create in them a feeling of affection and good-will towards one another; and, secondly, they would have no occasion to quarrel about their subsistence, for they would have pasture in abundance, except just at first, and in some particular cases; and from their pasture-land they would obtain the greater part of their food in a primitive age, having plenty of milk and flesh; moreover they would procure other food by the chase, not to be despised either in quantity or quality. They would also have abundance of clothing, and bedding, and dwellings, and utensils either capable of standing on the fire or not; for the plastic and weaving arts do not require any use of iron: and God has given these two arts to man in order to provide him with all such things, that, when reduced to the last extremity, the human race may still grow and increase.

Hence in those days mankind were not very poor; nor was poverty a cause of difference among them; and rich they could not have been, having neither gold nor silver: such at that time was their condition. And the community which has neither poverty nor riches will always have the noblest principles; in it there is no insolence or injustice, nor, again, are there any contentions or envyings. And therefore they were good, and also because they were what is called simple-minded; and when they were told about good and evil, they in their simplicity believed what they heard to be very truth and practised it. No one had the wit to suspect another of a falsehood, as men do now; but what they heard about Gods and men they believed to be true, and lived accordingly; and therefore they were in all respects such as we have described them."

"That quite accords with my views, and with those of my friend here."
refashioned:
"First, their isolation and vulnerability would have bred mutual affection and goodwill. Second, they would have had no reason to fight over resources. Pastureland would have been abundant -- with only rare exceptions early on -- providing ample milk and meat. Hunting would supplement their diet generously, both in quantity and quality. They would also have had sufficient clothing, bedding, shelter, and household implements, since pottery and weaving require no iron. God gave humanity these two crafts precisely so that even in the direst circumstances, the race could survive and multiply.

As a result, these early people were neither truly poor nor truly rich -- they had no gold or silver. A community without extremes of wealth or poverty will always cultivate the noblest character. Where there is no great wealth, there is no arrogance or injustice; where there is no poverty, there is no envy or strife. They were good people, partly because of these conditions, and partly because they were simple and trusting. When told what was good or evil, they believed it sincerely and lived accordingly. No one was cunning enough to suspect others of deception, as people do today. Whatever they heard about gods and human conduct, they accepted as truth and shaped their lives around it. This is why they were the kind of people we have described."

"That matches perfectly with what both I and my friend here believe."
elegant:
Isolation bred mutual affection, and abundance of pasture eliminated conflict over resources. Milk, meat, and game provided ample food. Pottery and weaving -- arts that need no iron -- supplied all other necessities, given by God precisely so humanity could survive even the worst catastrophe.

These people were neither poor nor rich, having no gold or silver. A community without extremes of wealth produces the noblest character -- free from arrogance, injustice, envy, and strife. Their simple-mindedness made them good: they believed what they were told about right and wrong and lived accordingly, with no capacity for deception or suspicion.
summary: The Athenian argues that post-catastrophe humans lived in a state of natural goodness. Mutual affection arose from shared vulnerability, conflict was absent because resources were abundant, and the basic arts of pottery and weaving sustained them without metal. Without wealth inequality, there was no injustice or envy. Their simplicity made them morally good -- they believed and practiced what they were taught without suspicion or deceit.
distilled: Primitive equality and simplicity produced natural goodness: without wealth disparity or sophistication, there was neither injustice nor deception.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias assents for himself and Megillus. -- This is one of Plato's most significant passages on the relationship between material conditions and moral character. The 'noble savage' portrait parallels the 'healthy city' of Republic 369b-372d before luxury corrupts it. The claim that God gave pottery and weaving as survival arts echoes the Protagoras myth (Prometheus/Epimetheus, 320d-322d) and Statesman 274a-d. The principle that communities without wealth extremes have the noblest character is central to the Laws' political program -- see Book 5 (735e-736a) on property limits. The connection between simplicity and moral goodness anticipates Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality. Compare also Hesiod's golden age in Works and Days 109-120.

narrative: "Would not many generations living on in a simple manner, although ruder, perhaps, and more ignorant of the arts generally, and in particular of those of land or naval warfare, and likewise of other arts, termed in cities legal practices and party conflicts, and including all conceivable ways of hurting one another in word and deed -- although inferior to those who lived before the deluge, or to the men of our day in these respects, would they not, I say, be simpler and more manly, and also more temperate and altogether more just? The reason has been already explained."
refashioned: "Would not many generations living simply in this way -- though cruder and more ignorant of the arts, especially those of land and naval warfare, and likewise ignorant of what cities call legal proceedings and partisan politics, including every conceivable method of harming one another through words and actions -- would they not, despite being inferior in these respects to both their pre-deluge ancestors and to people of our own time, be simpler, braver, more self-controlled, and on the whole more just? The reason for this has already been explained."
elegant: Many generations would have lived simply -- ignorant of warfare, litigation, political faction, and every sophisticated method of harming others. Though cruder than both their pre-catastrophe ancestors and modern people, they would have been simpler, braver, more temperate, and more just. The reason has already been given.
summary: The Athenian concludes that post-catastrophe generations, though less sophisticated than either their pre-deluge ancestors or contemporary people, would have been morally superior in simplicity, courage, temperance, and justice -- precisely because they lacked knowledge of warfare, legal manipulation, and political scheming.
distilled: Ignorance of sophisticated methods of harming others made primitive people simpler, braver, more temperate, and more just than their sophisticated descendants.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage crystallizes the paradox at the heart of Plato's political anthropology: moral virtue is inversely correlated with civilizational sophistication. The explicit linking of 'legal practices and party conflicts' with 'ways of hurting one another' is a sharp critique of Athenian democratic culture and the litigious society portrayed in works like Aristophanes' Wasps. The four virtues mentioned -- simplicity, manliness (courage), temperance, and justice -- echo the cardinal virtues of Republic 427e-434d, though here they arise naturally from material conditions rather than philosophical education. This complicates the Republic's claim that virtue requires philosophical knowledge.

narrative:
"Very true."

"I should wish you to understand that what has preceded and what is about to follow, has been, and will be said, with the intention of explaining what need the men of that time had of laws, and who was their lawgiver."

"And thus far what you have said has been very well said."

"They could hardly have wanted lawgivers as yet; nothing of that sort was likely to have existed in their days, for they had no letters at this early period; they lived by habit and the customs of their ancestors, as they are called."

"Probably."

"But there was already existing a form of government which, if I am not mistaken, is generally termed a lordship, and this still remains in many places, both among Hellenes and barbarians, and is the government which is declared by Homer to have prevailed among the Cyclopes:

'They have neither councils nor judgments, but they dwell in hollow caves on the tops of high mountains, and every one gives law to his wife and children, and they do not busy themselves about one another.'"

"That seems to be a charming poet of yours; I have read some other verses of his, which are very clever; but I do not know much of him, for foreign poets are very little read among the Cretans."

"But they are in Lacedaemon, and he appears to be the prince of them all; the manner of life, however, which he describes is not Spartan, but rather Ionian, and he seems quite to confirm what you are saying, when he traces up the ancient state of mankind by the help of tradition to barbarism."

"Yes, he does confirm it; and we may accept his witness to the fact that such forms of government sometimes arise."

"We may."
refashioned:
"Indeed."

"I want you to understand that everything I have said so far, and everything I am about to say, is aimed at one question: what need did people in those early times have for laws, and who served as their lawgiver?"

"So far your account has been excellent."

"They would have had no need for lawgivers yet. Nothing of the sort could have existed in their time, since they had no writing. They lived entirely by habit and ancestral custom."

"That seems likely."

"However, there was already a form of government -- what is generally called a patriarchal lordship. It still exists in many places, among both Greeks and non-Greeks. Homer describes exactly this arrangement among the Cyclopes: 'They have neither assemblies nor established laws, but dwell in hollow caves on mountaintops, and each one rules over his own wife and children, taking no concern for the others.'"

"He seems like a delightful poet of yours. I have read a few of his verses and found them quite skillful, but I do not know his work well -- foreign poets are rarely studied among us Cretans."

"In Sparta, however, Homer is highly regarded -- the greatest of all poets, in fact. The way of life he describes, though, is not Spartan but Ionian. Still, he confirms exactly what you are saying: that the most ancient human societies were what we would call primitive."

"Yes, he does confirm it. We can accept his testimony that such forms of government do arise."

"Agreed."
elegant:
The entire discussion aims at one question: what need did early humans have for laws, and who was their lawgiver?

At first, there were no lawgivers -- no writing existed. People lived by habit and ancestral custom. But a form of government already existed: patriarchal lordship, where each household head ruled his own family. Homer describes this among the Cyclopes, who had no assemblies or laws but dwelt in caves, each ruling his own household independently.

Homer -- little known in Crete but revered in Sparta -- confirms that the earliest human societies were primitive and fragmented.
summary: The Athenian frames his historical narrative as answering the question of when and why laws became necessary. Early post-catastrophe humans had no lawgivers or writing, living by habit and custom under patriarchal household rule -- a system Homer describes among the Cyclopes. A brief cultural exchange reveals Homer is little read in Crete but highly esteemed in Sparta.
distilled: Before formal laws existed, humans lived under patriarchal household rule -- the Cyclopes model Homer describes -- governed only by habit and ancestral custom.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, and Megillus all participate. -- The Homer quotation is from Odyssey 9.112-115, describing the Cyclopes. This is a key text in ancient Greek political thought about the state of nature. The charming exchange about Homer reveals cultural differences: Crete was known for its own strong literary traditions and relative insularity, while Sparta famously revered Homer (despite the irony that Homeric life is 'Ionian'). The term 'lordship' (dynasteia) as the first form of government parallels Aristotle's Politics 1252b (the household as the basic political unit). The absence of writing (grammata) as a condition of pre-legal society connects to the Phaedrus 274b-275b critique of writing. The Athenian's framing of the discussion as investigating the 'need for laws' establishes the teleological method of the entire Laws.

narrative: "And were not such states composed of men who had been dispersed in single habitations and families by the poverty which attended the devastations; and did not the eldest then rule among them, because with them government originated in the authority of a father and a mother, whom, like a flock of birds, they followed, forming one troop under the patriarchal rule and sovereignty of their parents, which of all sovereignties is the most just?"
refashioned: "Were not these earliest communities made up of people who had been scattered into isolated households and family units by the destitution that followed the catastrophe? And did not the eldest naturally assume authority, since government originated in the power of fathers and mothers -- whom the family members followed like a flock of birds, forming a single group under the patriarchal rule and sovereignty of their parents? And is not this the most just form of sovereignty?"
elegant: The earliest communities consisted of families scattered by catastrophe. The eldest ruled naturally, since government originated in parental authority. Like a flock of birds following their leader, families formed single units under patriarchal sovereignty -- the most just of all forms of rule.
summary: The Athenian describes the first political units as scattered families where the eldest ruled by natural parental authority. He compares these family groups to flocks of birds and declares patriarchal sovereignty the most just form of government.
distilled: Government originated in patriarchal family authority -- the most natural and just form of sovereignty.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The bird-flock analogy for patriarchal rule is vivid and unusual for Plato. The claim that parental authority is 'the most just' sovereignty reflects the natural hierarchy theme that runs through the Laws. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1252a-b, which similarly derives the polis from the household. The Statesman (267d-268a) uses the shepherd analogy for rulers; here the family leader is more like a lead bird -- suggesting natural rather than imposed authority. This passage grounds political legitimacy in nature (physis) rather than convention (nomos), taking a different position from the sophistic debate in Republic 338c-339a.

narrative:
"Very true."

"After this they came together in greater numbers, and increased the size of their cities, and betook themselves to husbandry, first of all at the foot of the mountains, and made enclosures of loose walls and works of defence, in order to keep off wild beasts; thus creating a single large and common habitation."

"Yes; at least we may suppose so."

"There is another thing which would probably happen."

"What?"
refashioned:
"Quite right."

"After this, people began coming together in larger groups, forming bigger settlements. They took up farming, starting at the foothills of the mountains, and built rough enclosures of loose stone walls and defensive works to protect against wild animals -- thus creating their first large, shared communities."

"Yes, we can reasonably suppose that is what happened."

"And there is something else that would likely have occurred."

"What is that?"
elegant: As populations grew, scattered families merged into larger settlements at the mountain foothills. They took up agriculture and built rough walls to defend against wild beasts -- creating the first shared communities.
summary: The Athenian describes the next stage of political development: families gradually came together into larger communities at the foothills, began practicing agriculture, and built defensive walls against wild animals, creating the first common settlements.
distilled: The transition from isolated families to shared settlements was driven by population growth, agriculture, and the need for collective defense.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias briefly assents; the Athenian Stranger narrates. -- This describes the second stage of political evolution: from isolated patriarchal households to village communities (komai). The progression -- mountain isolation, then foothill settlement, then plains -- mirrors archaeological patterns of post-catastrophe resettlement. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1252b on the village as a 'colony of the household.' The detail about loose stone walls (not requiring metallurgy) maintains internal consistency with the earlier point about lost technology. The transition from pastoral to agricultural life echoes Varro's De Re Rustica and later stadial theories of civilization.

narrative:
"When these larger habitations grew up out of the lesser original ones, each of the lesser ones would survive in the larger; every family would be under the rule of the eldest, and, owing to their separation from one another, would have peculiar customs in things divine and human, which they would have received from their several parents who had educated them; and these customs would incline them to order, when the parents had the element of order in their nature, and to courage, when they had the element of courage. And they would naturally stamp upon their children, and upon their children's children, their own likings; and, as we are saying, they would find their way into the larger society, having already their own peculiar laws."

"Certainly."

"And every man surely likes his own laws best, and the laws of others not so well."

"True."

"Then now we seem to have stumbled upon the beginnings of legislation."

"Exactly."
refashioned:
"As these larger communities formed from the smaller original ones, the smaller family units would persist within the larger group. Each family would still be governed by its eldest member and would maintain its own distinctive customs -- both religious and secular -- inherited from the parents who raised them. These customs would tend toward orderliness when the parents were naturally orderly, and toward courage when the parents were naturally brave. Parents would naturally impress their own preferences upon their children and grandchildren. So when these families entered the larger community, they brought with them their own established practices -- effectively, their own laws."

"That makes sense."

"And everyone naturally favors their own customs and regards the customs of others with less enthusiasm."

"True enough."

"So it seems we have arrived at the very beginnings of legislation."

"Precisely."
elegant:
When families merged into larger communities, each retained its own customs and internal authority. Religious and secular traditions were inherited from parents and grandparents, shaped by the character of each family line -- orderly families produced orderly customs, brave families produced brave ones.

Each family entered the larger society with its own established practices. Since everyone naturally prefers their own customs to those of others, the need to reconcile competing traditions marks the beginning of legislation.
summary: As families merged into larger communities, each brought its own inherited customs shaped by the character of its founding parents. The resulting diversity of traditions within a single settlement -- combined with everyone's natural preference for their own customs over others' -- created the conditions that necessitated formal legislation.
distilled: Legislation originated from the need to reconcile competing family customs when diverse households merged into larger communities.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger leads; Cleinias agrees. -- This is a crucial passage for understanding Plato's theory of legal origins: law arises not from a single legislator's wisdom but from the practical need to harmonize competing customary traditions. The idea that family character is 'stamped' on descendants through education parallels Republic 549c-550b (how regimes reproduce themselves through family upbringing). The observation that 'every man likes his own laws best' anticipates the concept of ethnocentrism and connects to Herodotus 3.38 (Darius's experiment showing each people prefers its own customs). This organic, bottom-up account of legal development contrasts with the top-down model of the philosopher-king in the Republic.

narrative: "The next step will be that these persons who have met together, will select some arbiters, who will review the laws of all of them, and will publicly present such as they approve to the chiefs who lead the tribes, and who are in a manner their kings, allowing them to choose those which they think best. These persons will themselves be called legislators, and will appoint the magistrates, framing some sort of aristocracy, or perhaps monarchy, out of the dynasties or lordships, and in this altered state of the government they will live."
refashioned: "The next step is that these assembled communities will choose a group of arbiters to review all their various customs and laws. These arbiters will publicly present the ones they approve to the tribal chiefs -- who are essentially their kings -- and allow them to select whichever they judge best. These arbiters will come to be called legislators. They will appoint magistrates and transform the old patriarchal lordships into something resembling an aristocracy or perhaps a monarchy. Under this new form of government, the people will continue their lives."
elegant: Communities will select arbiters to review and compare all existing customs, presenting the best to their tribal chiefs for approval. These arbiters become the first legislators. They appoint magistrates and reshape the old patriarchal lordships into aristocracies or monarchies -- the second stage of political development.
summary: The Athenian describes how diverse communities selected arbiters to review and harmonize competing customs, presenting the best laws to tribal chiefs for adoption. These arbiters became the first legislators, establishing magistrates and transforming patriarchal lordships into more formal aristocracies or monarchies.
distilled: The first legislators were arbiters chosen to review competing family customs and establish a unified legal code under aristocratic or monarchical authority.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This describes the transition from the first form of government (patriarchal lordship/dynasteia) to the second (aristocracy or monarchy). The process is remarkably democratic for Plato: arbiters are selected, they review laws publicly, and chiefs choose among them. This bottom-up, consensual account of institutional development contrasts with the top-down imposition of the philosopher-king in the Republic. The term 'legislators' (nomothetai) here denotes a specific political function that would become formalized in Athens's own constitutional history. Compare Aristotle's account of similar developments in Politics 1252b-1253a.

narrative:
"Yes, that would be the natural order of things."

"Then, now let us speak of a third form of government, in which all other forms and conditions of polities and cities concur."

"What is that?"

"The form which in fact Homer indicates as following the second. This third form arose when, as he says, Dardanus founded Dardania:

'For not as yet had the holy Ilium been built on the plain to be a city of speaking men; but they were still dwelling at the foot of many-fountained Ida.'

For indeed, in these verses, and in what he said of the Cyclopes, he speaks the words of God and nature; for poets are a divine race and often in their strains, by the aid of the Muses and the Graces, they attain truth."

"Yes."

"Then now let us proceed with the rest of our tale, which will probably be found to illustrate in some degree our proposed design. Shall we do so?"

"By all means."

"Ilium was built, when they descended from the mountain, in a large and fair plain, on a sort of low hill, watered by many rivers descending from Ida."

"Such is the tradition."

"And we must suppose this event to have taken place many ages after the deluge?"

"A marvellous forgetfulness of the former destruction would appear to have come over them, when they placed their town right under numerous streams flowing from the heights, trusting for their security to not very high hills, either."

"There must have been a long interval, clearly."

"And, as population increased, many other cities would begin to be inhabited."

"Doubtless."

"Those cities made war against Troy -- by sea as well as land -- for at that time men were ceasing to be afraid of the sea."

"Clearly."

"The Achaeans remained ten years, and overthrew Troy."

"True."
refashioned:
"Yes, that is the natural progression."

"Now let us consider a third form of government -- the one in which all other political forms and conditions converge."

"What form is that?"

"The one Homer indicates as coming after the second. It arose when Dardanus founded Dardania, as Homer tells us: 'For holy Ilium had not yet been built on the plain as a city of speaking men; they were still dwelling at the foot of many-fountained Ida.' In these verses, as in his account of the Cyclopes, Homer speaks with divine insight. Poets are a divinely inspired race, and through the aid of the Muses and Graces, they often arrive at truth."

"Indeed."

"Let us continue our story, which will likely shed light on our larger inquiry. Shall we?"

"Absolutely."

"Troy was eventually built after they descended from the mountains -- on a broad, beautiful plain, atop a low hill, watered by rivers flowing down from Mount Ida."

"So the tradition holds."

"This must have occurred many ages after the deluge."

"It is remarkable how completely they had forgotten the previous catastrophe -- building their city directly beneath streams flowing from the heights, and trusting their safety to hills that were not even very high."

"A great deal of time must have passed, clearly."

"As the population grew, many other cities would have sprung up as well."

"No doubt."

"These cities eventually made war against Troy -- by sea as well as by land -- for by that time, humanity had lost its fear of the ocean."

"Obviously."

"The Achaeans besieged Troy for ten years and finally destroyed it."

"True."
elegant:
The third form of government emerges as cities grow into full polities. Homer provides the evidence: Dardanus first settled at the foot of Mount Ida before Troy was built on the plain -- and poets, divinely inspired, often attain truth.

Troy was built on a low hill in a broad plain watered by rivers from Ida -- proof that by then, people had completely forgotten the ancient deluge. As populations expanded, many cities arose. Eventually they made war on Troy by land and sea, for humanity had overcome its fear of the ocean. After ten years, the Achaeans destroyed Troy.
summary: The Athenian describes the third stage of political evolution: the founding of full cities on the plains, exemplified by Troy. Homer's account of Dardanus settling near Ida before Troy was built on the plain is treated as divinely inspired evidence. The fact that Troy was built in a flood-prone location shows that memory of the great catastrophe had faded. Population growth led to the proliferation of cities and eventually to the Trojan War.
distilled: The third political stage -- full cities on the plains -- emerged as catastrophe was forgotten, culminating in the network of states that waged the Trojan War.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger alternate. -- The Homer quotation is from Iliad 20.216-218. The claim that 'poets are a divine race' who 'attain truth' is notable given Plato's famously critical stance toward poetry in Republic 595a-608b; here in the Laws the attitude is markedly more generous, treating poets as valid historical witnesses. The three stages of political evolution now described -- (1) patriarchal household, (2) aristocratic/monarchical village confederation, (3) full city-state -- constitute a sophisticated developmental schema. The observation about 'forgetfulness of the former destruction' introduces the theme of collective amnesia that enables both progress and repeated error. The Trojan War serves as the historical anchor connecting mythic prehistory to the Dorian settlement that follows.

narrative:
"And during the ten years in which the Achaeans were besieging Ilium, the homes of the besiegers were falling into an evil plight. Their youth revolted; and when the soldiers returned to their own cities and families, they did not receive them properly, and as they ought to have done, and numerous deaths, murders, exiles, were the consequence. The exiles came again, under a new name, no longer Achaeans, but Dorians -- a name which they derived from Dorieus; for it was he who gathered them together. The rest of the story is told by you Lacedaemonians as part of the history of Sparta."

"To be sure."
refashioned:
"During the ten years the Achaeans spent besieging Troy, their homelands fell into serious trouble. The younger generation revolted against authority. When the soldiers finally returned to their cities and families, they were not welcomed back properly -- as they should have been. The result was widespread violence: deaths, murders, and exiles. The exiled groups eventually reconstituted themselves under a new identity. They were no longer called Achaeans but Dorians -- a name taken from Dorieus, who gathered them together. The rest of this story, of course, you Spartans know as part of your own history."

"Indeed we do."
elegant: During the ten-year siege of Troy, the besiegers' homelands fell into disorder -- the youth revolted. Returning soldiers were badly received, leading to murders and exiles. The exiles regrouped under the name Dorians, after Dorieus who united them. The rest belongs to Spartan history.
summary: The Athenian explains that during the prolonged siege of Troy, social order collapsed in the Achaean homelands. Returning soldiers faced hostility rather than welcome, resulting in widespread violence and exile. These exiles reformed as the Dorians, named after their leader Dorieus, connecting the mythic Trojan War era to the historical Dorian settlement that founded Sparta.
distilled: The Trojan War's prolonged absence caused domestic upheaval, producing the exiles who became the Dorians and ultimately founded Sparta.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus confirms. -- This passage bridges Homeric mythology and historical tradition. The account of domestic upheaval during the Trojan War parallels Thucydides 1.12 on the post-war troubles (nostoi). The Dorian invasion (or migration) was a central event in Greek historical consciousness, traditionally dated to the 12th-11th centuries BCE. The etymology connecting 'Dorians' to 'Dorieus' follows one ancient tradition. By deferring to Megillus for the Spartan portion, the Athenian shows diplomatic courtesy and acknowledges that local traditions carry authority. This passage also implicitly critiques prolonged foreign military campaigns -- a theme relevant to Athens's own experience with the Sicilian Expedition.

narrative: "Thus, after digressing from the original subject of laws into music and drinking-bouts, the argument has, providentially, come back to the same point, and presents to us another handle. For we have reached the settlement of Lacedaemon; which, as you truly say, is in laws and in institutions the sister of Crete. And we are all the better for the digression, because we have gone through various governments and settlements, and have been present at the foundation of a first, second, and third state, succeeding one another in infinite time. And now there appears on the horizon a fourth state or nation which was once in process of settlement and has continued settled to this day. If, out of all this, we are able to discern what is well or ill settled, and what laws are the salvation and what are the destruction of cities, and what changes would make a state happy, O Megillus and Cleinias, we may now begin again, unless we have some fault to find with the previous discussion."
refashioned: "So our argument, after its seeming digression from laws into topics like music and drinking customs, has providentially circled back to the same point and given us a fresh angle of approach. We have arrived at the founding of Sparta -- which, as you rightly observe, is the sister of Crete in its laws and institutions. The digression has actually served us well, because we have traced the development of several forms of government, witnessing the foundation of a first, second, and third type of state, each succeeding the other across vast stretches of time. Now a fourth form of state appears before us -- one that was established in the past and has endured to the present day. If from all this we can determine what makes a state well or poorly ordered, which laws preserve cities and which destroy them, and what changes lead to a state's happiness -- then, Megillus and Cleinias, we are ready to begin our real work, unless you find fault with the discussion so far."
elegant:
The argument has circled back productively from its digression into music and drinking. We have traced three successive forms of government across vast time, and now a fourth appears -- Sparta, sister of Crete in law and institutions, which has endured to the present.

The goal is now clear: to determine from this history what makes states well or poorly governed, which laws preserve or destroy cities, and what changes produce happiness.
summary: The Athenian summarizes the discussion's progress: having traced three stages of political development from post-catastrophe patriarchy through aristocratic confederation to full city-states, they have arrived at Sparta -- the fourth and enduring form. He frames the purpose of this historical survey as determining what laws preserve cities and what laws destroy them.
distilled: Having traced three forms of government through history, the discussion arrives at Sparta as the enduring fourth form, from which to learn what laws save or destroy cities.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The reference to 'music and drinking-bouts' recalls Books 1-2, which discussed Cretan and Spartan drinking customs and musical education. The description of Sparta as the 'sister of Crete' in laws and institutions reflects the widespread ancient belief (also in Aristotle, Politics 1271b) that Lycurgus modeled Spartan institutions on Cretan ones. The four-stage developmental scheme (patriarchal household, village aristocracy, city-state, Dorian settlement) provides the historical scaffolding for the entire Laws. The language of 'salvation and destruction of cities' echoes the central concern of the Republic. The address to both Megillus and Cleinias signals that the argument now enters territory relevant to both their states.

narrative:
"If some God, Stranger, would promise us that our new enquiry about legislation would be as good and full as the present, I would go a great way to hear such another, and would think that a day as long as this -- and we are now approaching the longest day of the year -- was too short for the discussion."

"Then I suppose that we must consider this subject?"

"Certainly."

"Let us place ourselves in thought at the moment when Lacedaemon and Argos and Messene and the rest of the Peloponnesus were all in complete subjection, Megillus, to your ancestors; for afterwards, as the legend informs us, they divided their army into three portions, and settled three cities, Argos, Messene, Lacedaemon."

"True."

"Temenus was the king of Argos, Cresphontes of Messene, Procles and Eurysthenes of Lacedaemon."

"Certainly."

"To these kings all the men of that day made oath that they would assist them, if any one subverted their kingdom."

"True."

"But can a kingship be destroyed, or was any other form of government ever destroyed, by any but the rulers themselves? No indeed, by Zeus. Have we already forgotten what was said a little while ago?"

"No."
refashioned:
"If some god, Stranger, could promise that our next investigation of legislation would be as rich and thorough as what we have done so far, I would travel a great distance to hear it. I would consider even a day as long as this one -- and we are now approaching the summer solstice -- too short for the conversation."

"Then we must press on with the subject?"

"We must."

"Let us place ourselves mentally at the moment when Sparta, Argos, Messene, and the rest of the Peloponnese were all under the dominion of your ancestors, Megillus. According to tradition, they then divided their forces into three parts and established three cities: Argos, Messene, and Sparta."

"That is correct."

"Temenus became king of Argos, Cresphontes of Messene, and the twin brothers Procles and Eurysthenes of Sparta."

"Yes."

"And all the people of that time swore oaths to these kings, pledging to defend them if anyone attempted to overthrow their rule."

"Correct."

"But can a kingdom be destroyed -- can any form of government be destroyed -- by anyone other than its own rulers? By Zeus, it cannot. Have we already forgotten what we said just recently about this?"

"We have not."
elegant:
Megillus enthusiastically endorses continuing the inquiry, saying even the longest day of the year would be too short for such a discussion.

The Athenian asks them to consider the moment when the Dorian conquerors divided the Peloponnese into three kingdoms: Argos under Temenus, Messene under Cresphontes, and Sparta under Procles and Eurysthenes. All the people swore mutual oaths of protection. Yet the critical principle remains: no government is ever destroyed except by its own rulers.
summary: After Megillus eagerly endorses continuing the discussion, the Athenian turns to the Dorian settlement of the Peloponnese: the three kingdoms of Argos, Messene, and Sparta were established with mutual oaths of protection. He reintroduces the key principle that governments are only ever destroyed from within, by their own rulers.
distilled: The three Dorian kingdoms of Argos, Messene, and Sparta were founded with mutual defense oaths, yet governments are only destroyed by their own rulers.
notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The mention of the 'longest day of the year' provides a dramatic frame -- the conversation occurs near the summer solstice, echoing the temporal setting of the Republic (set during a festival). The historical detail about the Heraclid division of the Peloponnese follows standard Greek tradition (cf. Apollodorus, Bibliotheca 2.8). The three royal houses: Temenus (Argos), Cresphontes (Messene), and the dual kingship of Procles and Eurysthenes (Sparta) represent the Dorian lineages. The principle that governments are destroyed by their own rulers is central to the Laws' political theory and echoes Republic 545d-546a on how the ideal state would decline. The oath of mutual assistance anticipates modern concepts of collective security.

narrative: "And may we not now further confirm what was then mentioned? For we have come upon facts which have brought us back again to the same principle; so that, in resuming the discussion, we shall not be enquiring about an empty theory, but about events which actually happened. The case was as follows: Three royal heroes made oath to three cities which were under a kingly government, and the cities to the kings, that both rulers and subjects should govern and be governed according to the laws which were common to all of them: the rulers promised that as time and the race went forward they would not make their rule more arbitrary; and the subjects said that, if the rulers observed these conditions, they would never subvert or permit others to subvert those kingdoms; the kings were to assist kings and peoples when injured, and the peoples were to assist peoples and kings in like manner. Is not this the fact?"
refashioned: "Can we not now confirm what we said earlier with concrete evidence? We have arrived at historical events that illustrate the very same principle, so our discussion will no longer concern mere theory but actual occurrences. Here is what happened: Three royal leaders swore oaths to the three cities under their rule, and the cities swore oaths in return. Both sides pledged that rulers and subjects alike would govern and be governed according to laws common to all three states. The rulers promised that over time they would not make their power more despotic. The subjects pledged that as long as the rulers kept their word, they would never overthrow -- or allow anyone else to overthrow -- these kingdoms. Kings were to assist other kings and peoples when they suffered injustice, and peoples were to assist other peoples and kings in the same way. Is this not what happened?"
elegant: Theory is now confirmed by history. Three kings swore mutual oaths with three cities: rulers and subjects would abide by common laws. The kings promised never to increase their power toward despotism. The subjects promised never to overthrow -- or permit the overthrow of -- the kingdoms, so long as the kings kept their word. Both kings and peoples pledged mutual assistance against injustice.
summary: The Athenian details the mutual constitutional compact between the three Dorian kingdoms: kings swore not to become more despotic over time, subjects swore loyalty conditional on this restraint, and all parties pledged mutual military assistance. This historical arrangement, he argues, confirms the theoretical principles discussed earlier.
distilled: The Dorian compact established a constitutional bargain: kings would not become despotic, subjects would remain loyal, and all three kingdoms would defend each other.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage describes what amounts to a constitutional contract or social compact -- remarkably anticipating Enlightenment contractarian theory (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau). The key elements are: (1) mutual obligation between rulers and ruled, (2) a promise against increasing arbitrary power, (3) conditional loyalty, and (4) collective security among allied states. The emphasis on the rulers' promise not to make their rule 'more arbitrary' over time addresses the central problem of constitutional decay. Compare Republic 562a-563e on how democracy degenerates into tyranny. The phrase 'not an empty theory but events which actually happened' reflects the Laws' distinctive method of grounding political philosophy in historical analysis rather than purely theoretical construction.

narrative:
"Yes."

"And the three states to whom these laws were given, whether their kings or any others were the authors of them, had therefore the greatest security for the maintenance of their constitutions?"

"What security?"

"That the other two states were always to come to the rescue against a rebellious third."

"True."

"Many persons say that legislators ought to impose such laws as the mass of the people will be ready to receive; but this is just as if one were to command gymnastic masters or physicians to treat or cure their pupils or patients in an agreeable manner."

"Exactly."

"Whereas the physician may often be too happy if he can restore health, and make the body whole, without any very great infliction of pain."

"Certainly."

"There was also another advantage possessed by the men of that day, which greatly lightened the task of passing laws."

"What advantage?"
refashioned:
"Indeed."

"So these three states, regardless of whether their kings or others authored the laws, possessed the greatest possible security for maintaining their constitutions?"

"What kind of security?"

"The assurance that if any one of the three states rebelled against the compact, the other two would come to its defense."

"That is right."

"Now, many people argue that legislators should only impose laws that the general population is willing to accept. But that is like telling a gym trainer or doctor to treat their students or patients only in pleasant ways."

"Precisely."

"In reality, a physician is often fortunate if he can restore health and make the body whole without inflicting too much pain."

"Of course."

"The people of that era also had another advantage that made the task of lawmaking much easier."

"What advantage was that?"
elegant:
The three-state alliance provided the ultimate constitutional guarantee: two states would always defend against a rebellious third.

The common demand that legislators only pass popular laws is like telling doctors to cure patients only through pleasant treatments. A good physician is fortunate to restore health without excessive pain.

The Dorian settlers also had a special advantage that made lawmaking easier.
summary: The Athenian identifies the three-state mutual defense pact as the strongest possible constitutional safeguard. He then attacks the populist notion that laws should only be what people want to hear, comparing it to telling doctors to only use pleasant treatments. He also notes that the Dorian settlers had a unique advantage for legislation.
distilled: Constitutional security came from mutual defense among three allied states, and good legislation -- like good medicine -- cannot always be pleasant.
notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The physician analogy for the legislator is a recurring Platonic motif: see Statesman 293a-c, Gorgias 464b-465d, and Republic 389b. The argument that law should not be constrained by popular acceptability directly challenges democratic principles and reflects the Laws' consistent preference for expert governance. The mutual defense arrangement anticipates the logic of NATO-style collective security alliances. The 'advantage' the Athenian is about to reveal (equal property distribution, no debts) connects to one of the most contentious issues in Greek politics -- land reform and debt cancellation, which drove revolutions in many city-states.

narrative: "The legislators of that day, when they equalized property, escaped the great accusation which generally arises in legislation, if a person attempts to disturb the possession of land, or to abolish debts, because he sees that without this reform there can never be any real equality. Now, in general, when the legislator attempts to make a new settlement of such matters, every one meets him with the cry, that 'he is not to disturb vested interests' -- declaring with imprecations that he is introducing agrarian laws and cancelling of debts, until a man is at his wits end; whereas no one could quarrel with the Dorians for distributing the land -- there was nothing to hinder them; and as for debts, they had none which were considerable or of old standing."
refashioned: "The lawmakers of that era, by equalizing property from the start, avoided the greatest obstacle that legislators typically face: the fierce opposition that arises whenever anyone tries to redistribute land or cancel debts, even when they can see that without such reform, genuine equality is impossible. Normally, when a legislator attempts to restructure these matters, everyone protests that he must not tamper with established property rights. They hurl curses at him for proposing land redistribution and debt forgiveness, until the poor man is driven to desperation. But no one could object to the Dorians distributing land equally -- there were no prior claims to dispute. And as for debts, they had none of any significance or long standing."
elegant: By equalizing property at the outset, the Dorian lawmakers avoided the greatest obstacle in legislation: the furious opposition to land redistribution and debt cancellation. Normally, any attempt to reform these matters meets with outrage over 'vested interests,' even though true equality requires it. But the Dorians faced no such resistance -- there were no established property claims to disturb and no significant debts to cancel.
summary: The Athenian explains that the Dorian legislators had the rare advantage of being able to equalize property without opposition, since they were starting fresh with no established land claims or debts. In contrast, any normal legislator who attempts redistribution faces furious resistance from entrenched interests, even when equality is impossible without reform.
distilled: The Dorians could equalize property without opposition because they started fresh, unlike later legislators who face furious resistance from vested interests.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage addresses one of the most explosive issues in Greek politics. Land redistribution (ge anadasmos) and debt cancellation (chreon apokope) were the rallying cries of revolutionary movements throughout the Greek world, from Solon's seisachtheia in Athens (594 BCE) to the reforms of Agis IV and Cleomenes III in Sparta (3rd century BCE). The observation that vested interests block necessary reform is remarkably modern. Compare Laws 684d-e and 736c-e where Plato further discusses the difficulty of property reform. The Dorians' advantage -- founding a new settlement with no prior claims -- is precisely the condition Plato seeks to replicate with his new colony in Magnesia (Books 5-6). See also Republic 462b-c on communal property.

narrative:
"Very true."

"But then, my good friends, why did the settlement and legislation of their country turn out so badly?"

"How do you mean; and why do you blame them?"

"There were three kingdoms, and of these, two quickly corrupted their original constitution and laws, and the only one which remained was the Spartan."

"The question which you ask is not easily answered."

"And yet must be answered when we are enquiring about laws, this being our old man's sober game of play, whereby we beguile the way, as I was saying when we first set out on our journey."

"Certainly; and we must find out why this was."

"What laws are more worthy of our attention than those which have regulated such cities? or what settlements of states are greater or more famous?"

"I know of none."
refashioned:
"Quite true."

"But then, my friends, why did these settlements and their legislation turn out so badly?"

"What do you mean? Why do you fault them?"

"Of the three kingdoms, two quickly corrupted their original constitutions and laws. Only Sparta endured."

"That is not an easy question to answer."

"Yet it must be answered, since we are investigating laws -- this being, as I said when we started our journey, the sober game that old men play to pass the time."

"Indeed, and we must discover why this happened."

"What laws deserve our study more than those that governed such important states? What political settlements in history have been greater or more famous?"

"I cannot think of any."
elegant:
Despite every advantage, why did the Dorian settlement fail? Two of the three kingdoms quickly corrupted their constitutions; only Sparta endured.

This question demands an answer from anyone investigating laws. No greater or more famous political settlements exist to study. The inquiry itself is the dignified pastime of old men -- a sober game to pass the journey.
summary: The Athenian poses the central puzzle: despite ideal conditions, two of the three Dorian kingdoms rapidly corrupted their constitutions while only Sparta survived. This demands explanation from anyone seriously studying legislation. He characterizes the entire inquiry as the 'sober game' of elderly men beguiling a long journey.
distilled: The central puzzle of Dorian history is why two of three kingdoms with every advantage failed, while only Sparta endured.
notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The 'old man's sober game of play' (paidia) recalls the Laws' opening framework: three elderly men walking from Cnossos to the cave of Zeus on a long summer day. The characterization of philosophical inquiry as a kind of dignified play connects to the Platonic theme of serious playfulness (spoudaiogeloion). The failure of Argos and Messene despite ideal starting conditions is the historical test case for Plato's political theory. Messene was conquered by Sparta in the Messenian Wars (8th-7th centuries BCE) and its population reduced to helot status. Argos declined through internal faction. Only Sparta maintained its constitution -- attributed to Lycurgus -- for centuries. This pattern of constitutional decay echoes Republic 546a-547a.

narrative: "Can we doubt that your ancestors intended these institutions not only for the protection of Peloponnesus, but of all the Hellenes, in case they were attacked by the barbarian? For the inhabitants of the region about Ilium, when they provoked by their insolence the Trojan war, relied upon the power of the Assyrians and the Empire of Ninus, which still existed and had a great prestige; the people of those days fearing the united Assyrian Empire just as we now fear the Great King. And the second capture of Troy was a serious offence against them, because Troy was a portion of the Assyrian Empire. To meet the danger the single army was distributed between three cities by the royal brothers, sons of Heracles -- a fair device, as it seemed, and a far better arrangement than the expedition against Troy. For, firstly, the people of that day had, as they thought, in the Heraclidae better leaders than the Pelopidae; in the next place, they considered that their army was superior in valour to that which went against Troy; for, although the latter conquered the Trojans, they were themselves conquered by the Heraclidae -- Achaeans by Dorians. May we not suppose that this was the intention with which the men of those days framed the constitutions of their states?"
refashioned: "Can we doubt that your ancestors designed these institutions to protect not just the Peloponnese but all of Greece against barbarian invasion? Consider the context: the peoples around Troy, when they provoked the Trojan War through their arrogance, were relying on the power of the Assyrian Empire under Ninus, which still commanded enormous prestige. People feared that unified Assyrian power just as we today fear the Great King of Persia. The destruction of Troy was a serious provocation against Assyria, since Troy was part of their empire. To counter this threat, the single Dorian army was divided among three cities by the royal brothers, the sons of Heracles -- a strategy that seemed excellent and far superior to the Trojan War expedition. First, the people believed the Heraclidae were better leaders than the Pelopidae had been. Second, they considered their army more valiant than the one that fought at Troy -- after all, though the Achaeans conquered Troy, the Dorians had in turn conquered the Achaeans. We can reasonably suppose this was the strategic thinking behind the constitutions they established."
elegant: The Dorian settlement was designed to protect all of Greece against barbarian invasion. Troy had relied on the Assyrian Empire, which the Greeks feared as they now fear Persia. Destroying Troy provoked Assyria, so the Dorian army was strategically divided among three cities -- a better arrangement than the Trojan expedition. The Heraclidae were considered superior leaders to the Pelopidae, and the Dorian army more valiant: the Achaeans conquered Troy, but the Dorians conquered the Achaeans.
summary: The Athenian argues that the three-kingdom Dorian settlement was strategically designed to defend all of Greece against barbarian invasion, particularly the Assyrian Empire that had backed Troy. The division of forces among three cities was considered superior to the earlier Trojan War expedition, with the Heraclidae deemed better leaders and the Dorian army more valiant than the Achaeans.
distilled: The Dorian three-kingdom system was a strategic defense arrangement for all Greece, considered superior to the Trojan War expedition in both leadership and military power.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The identification of Troy with the Assyrian Empire is historically dubious but reflects Greek awareness of Near Eastern power structures. The 'Empire of Ninus' refers to the legendary Assyrian king Ninus, associated with Nineveh. The parallel between ancient Assyria and contemporary Persia ('the Great King') grounds the historical analysis in present political concerns -- the Persian threat remained real in Plato's lifetime. The comparison of Pelopidae (Agamemnon's line) and Heraclidae (the Dorian royal houses) uses the mythological genealogies that structured Greek political identity. The logic that the Dorians must be superior because they conquered the Achaeans who conquered Troy is a chain-of-conquest argument. Compare Thucydides 1.12 on the Dorian migration.

narrative:
"Quite true."

"And would not men who had shared with one another many dangers, and were governed by a single race of royal brothers, and had taken the advice of oracles, and in particular of the Delphian Apollo, be likely to think that such states would be firmly and lastingly established?"

"Of course they would."

"Yet these institutions, of which such great expectations were entertained, seem to have all rapidly vanished away; with the exception, as I was saying, of that small part of them which existed in your land. And this third part has never to this day ceased warring against the two others; whereas, if the original idea had been carried out, and they had agreed to be one, their power would have been invincible in war."

"No doubt."

"But what was the ruin of this glorious confederacy? Here is a subject well worthy of consideration."

"Certainly, no one will ever find more striking instances of laws or governments being the salvation or destruction of great and noble interests, than are here presented to his view."

"Then now we seem to have happily arrived at a real and important question."

"Very true."

"Did you never remark, sage friend, that all men, and we ourselves at this moment, often fancy that they see some beautiful thing which might have effected wonders if any one had only known how to make a right use of it in some way; and yet this mode of looking at things may turn out after all to be a mistake, and not according to nature, either in our own case or in any other?"

"To what are you referring, and what do you mean?"

"I was thinking of my own admiration of the aforesaid Heracleid expedition, which was so noble, and might have had such wonderful results for the Hellenes, if only rightly used; and I was just laughing at myself."

"But were you not right and wise in speaking as you did, and we in assenting to you?"

"Perhaps; and yet I cannot help observing that any one who sees anything great or powerful, immediately has the feeling that -- 'If the owner only knew how to use his great and noble possession, how happy would he be, and what great results would he achieve!'"

"And would he not be justified?"
refashioned:
"Quite so."

"And would not people who had faced many dangers together, who were ruled by a single royal family of brothers, and who had followed the guidance of oracles -- especially the oracle of Apollo at Delphi -- naturally assume that their states would be firmly and permanently established?"

"They certainly would."

"Yet these institutions, in which such great hopes were invested, all rapidly disintegrated -- except for the small portion that survived in your homeland, Sparta. And even that surviving part has never stopped warring against the other two. Had the original vision been carried out and they had remained united, their combined power would have been unbeatable."

"Undoubtedly."

"So what destroyed this magnificent alliance? That question is well worth investigating."

"Indeed -- one could hardly find more vivid examples of laws and governments either saving or ruining great and noble enterprises."

"We seem to have arrived at a genuinely important question."

"We have."

"But have you ever noticed, my wise friend, that people -- including ourselves right now -- often look at something impressive and think: 'If only someone knew how to use this properly, what wonders it could achieve!' And yet that way of thinking may itself be a mistake, not in accord with the nature of things?"

"What exactly do you mean?"

"I was reflecting on my own admiration for the Dorian expedition -- so noble, so full of potential for all of Greece, if only it had been used wisely. And I found myself laughing at my own reaction."

"But surely you were right to speak as you did, and we were right to agree?"

"Perhaps. Yet I keep noticing that whenever anyone sees something great and powerful, the immediate reaction is: 'If only the owner knew how to use this magnificent possession, how happy he would be and what extraordinary things he could accomplish!'"

"And is that reaction not justified?"
elegant:
