People who had shared dangers, followed oracles, and were governed by a royal brotherhood naturally expected their alliance to last forever. Yet it disintegrated rapidly -- only Sparta survived, and it never stopped fighting the other two. United, they would have been invincible.

What ruined this confederacy? The question is critical. But the Athenian catches himself: our instinct to see great power and think 'if only it were used wisely!' may itself be a mistake. Admiring potential is not the same as understanding what nature actually allows.
summary: Despite shared dangers, oracular guidance, and royal kinship, the Dorian confederacy rapidly collapsed -- only Sparta survived, perpetually at war with the others. The Athenian raises a deeper philosophical point: our tendency to admire great power and imagine what it could achieve 'if only used wisely' may itself be a fundamental error about human nature.
distilled: The Dorian confederacy's collapse despite every advantage reveals that admiring power's potential is not the same as understanding why power inevitably corrupts.
notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger alternate. -- The reference to the Delphian Apollo reflects the central role of the Delphic oracle in legitimizing Dorian claims to the Peloponnese (cf. Herodotus 6.52). The Athenian's self-correcting moment -- laughing at his own admiration -- is characteristically Socratic: the examined life requires questioning one's own assumptions. The insight that seeing great power and wishing it were 'used well' may be a mistake 'not according to nature' anticipates the argument that power itself tends to corrupt. Compare Republic 344a-345e (Thrasymachus on power) and the famous Acton dictum. The 'if only' fallacy -- assuming good outcomes from great power given proper use -- is precisely what the Laws seeks to overcome through institutional design rather than reliance on individual virtue.

narrative: "Reflect; in what point of view does this sort of praise appear just: First, in reference to the question in hand: If the then commanders had known how to arrange their army properly, how would they have attained success? Would not this have been the way? They would have bound them all firmly together and preserved them for ever, giving them freedom and dominion at pleasure, combined with the power of doing in the whole world, Hellenic and barbarian, whatever they and their descendants desired. What other aim would they have had?"
refashioned: "Consider from what perspective this kind of admiration seems justified. Take the case at hand: if the Dorian commanders had known how to properly organize their forces, how would they have achieved success? Presumably they would have bound all three kingdoms firmly together and maintained that unity forever, granting their peoples freedom and dominion as they wished, along with the power to accomplish whatever they and their descendants desired throughout the entire Greek and barbarian world. What other goal could they possibly have had?"
elegant: If the Dorian commanders had known how to properly organize their alliance, they would have bound the three kingdoms together permanently -- achieving freedom, dominion, and the power to do whatever they wished throughout the entire world. What other goal could there be?
summary: The Athenian frames the hypothetical: had the Dorian commanders known how to properly organize their alliance, they would have achieved permanent unity, freedom, and dominion over the entire Greek and barbarian world. This sets up his argument about what actually prevents such ideal outcomes.
distilled: The Dorian dream was permanent unity yielding absolute freedom and universal dominion -- but knowing how to achieve it was the real challenge.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage articulates the universal political fantasy: permanent power, complete freedom, and the ability to do whatever one desires. The Athenian states it so starkly precisely to expose its impossibility. The phrase 'whatever they and their descendants desired' echoes the critique of unconstrained desire that runs through the Laws. Compare Gorgias 466b-468e, where Socrates argues that tyrants who do whatever they wish actually have the least real power. The mention of 'Hellenic and barbarian' together as the scope of potential dominion reflects the pan-Hellenic ambitions that Alexander would later attempt to realize.

narrative:
"Very good."

"Suppose any one were in the same way to express his admiration at the sight of great wealth or family honour, or the like, he would praise them under the idea that through them he would attain either all or the greater and chief part of what he desires."

"He would."

"Well, now, and does not the argument show that there is one common desire of all mankind?"

"What is it?"

"The desire which a man has, that all things, if possible -- at any rate, things human -- may come to pass in accordance with his soul's desire."

"Certainly."

"And having this desire always, and at every time of life, in youth, in manhood, in age, he cannot help always praying for the fulfilment of it."

"No doubt."

"And we join in the prayers of our friends, and ask for them what they ask for themselves."

"We do."

"Dear is the son to the father -- the younger to the elder."

"Of course."

"And yet the son often prays to obtain things which the father prays that he may not obtain."

"When the son is young and foolish, you mean?"

"Yes; or when the father, in the dotage of age or the heat of youth, having no sense of right and justice, prays with fervour, under the influence of feelings akin to those of Theseus when he cursed the unfortunate Hippolytus, do you imagine that the son, having a sense of right and justice, will join in his father's prayers?"

"I understand you to mean that a man should not desire or be in a hurry to have all things according to his wish, for his wish may be at variance with his reason. But every state and every individual ought to pray and strive for wisdom."
refashioned:
"Very well."

"Similarly, when anyone admires great wealth, or noble lineage, or similar advantages, they praise these things because they imagine that through them they would obtain everything -- or at least the greater part of everything -- they desire."

"They would."

"But doesn't this reveal a single universal desire shared by all humanity?"

"What desire is that?"

"The desire that everything -- or at least all human affairs -- should unfold according to one's own wishes."

"Certainly."

"And since we carry this desire at every stage of life -- in youth, in maturity, in old age -- we cannot help perpetually praying for its fulfillment."

"Of course."

"We even join in our friends' prayers, asking for what they themselves ask."

"We do."

"A father holds his son dear, and the elder naturally cares for the younger."

"Naturally."

"Yet a son often prays for things the father prays he will not get."

"You mean when the son is young and foolish?"

"Yes -- or consider the reverse: when a father, whether in the recklessness of youth or the senility of old age, prays passionately for something unjust, driven by feelings like those of Theseus when he cursed his innocent son Hippolytus. Would the son, if he possesses a sense of justice, join in his father's prayer?"

"I see your point: a person should not rush to have everything go according to their wishes, because those wishes may conflict with reason. What every individual and every state should truly pray and strive for is wisdom."
elegant:
All humans share one universal desire: that everything should unfold according to their wishes. We carry this desire at every age, pray for it constantly, and even join our friends' prayers for it.

But wishes can conflict with reason. A son prays for things his father opposes; a father like Theseus, driven by passion, curses his own innocent son. The lesson: one should not desire that all wishes be fulfilled, because wishes may contradict justice. What individuals and states should truly pursue is wisdom.
summary: The Athenian identifies the universal human desire to have everything accord with one's wishes. But he demonstrates through the example of Theseus cursing Hippolytus that passionate desires can be at war with reason and justice. The conclusion is that neither individuals nor states should pray for the fulfillment of every wish, but rather for wisdom.
distilled: The universal desire to have everything accord with one's wishes is dangerous because wishes often conflict with reason -- the true object of prayer should be wisdom.
notes: Speaker: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The Theseus-Hippolytus story was famously dramatized in Euripides' Hippolytus (428 BCE): Theseus, believing false accusations against his son, used one of three wishes granted by Poseidon to curse Hippolytus to death. It exemplifies how passionate conviction can produce catastrophic injustice. The argument that universal desire must be governed by wisdom recapitulates the Republic's central thesis (441d-442d) about reason ruling the soul. The political analogy is direct: just as a father's unjust prayer should not bind his son, a ruler's unjust wishes should not bind the state. Megillus's concluding formulation -- 'every state and every individual ought to pray and strive for wisdom' -- demonstrates that he has grasped the philosophical point. Compare Alcibiades I 133c on praying for what is truly good.

narrative: "Yes; and I remember, and you will remember, what I said at first, that a statesman and legislator ought to ordain laws with a view to wisdom; while you were arguing that the good lawgiver ought to order all with a view to war. And to this I replied that there were four virtues, but that upon your view one of them only was the aim of legislation; whereas you ought to regard all virtue, and especially that which comes first, and is the leader of all the rest -- I mean wisdom and mind and opinion, having affection and desire in their train. And now the argument returns to the same point, and I say once more, in jest if you like, or in earnest if you like, that the prayer of a fool is full of danger, being likely to end in the opposite of what he desires. And if you would rather receive my words in earnest, I am willing that you should; and you will find, I suspect, as I have said already, that not cowardice was the cause of the ruin of the Dorian kings and of their whole design, nor ignorance of military matters, either on the part of the rulers or of their subjects; but their misfortunes were due to their general degeneracy, and especially to their ignorance of the most important human affairs. That was then, and is still, and always will be the case, as I will endeavour, if you will allow me, to make out and demonstrate as well as I am able to you who are my friends, in the course of the argument."
refashioned:
"Yes, and you will recall what I said at the beginning: a statesman and legislator should frame laws with a view to wisdom. You were arguing that the good lawgiver should orient everything toward war. I responded that there are four virtues, but on your view, legislation aimed at only one -- courage. The legislator should regard all the virtues, and especially the one that leads the rest: wisdom, which encompasses intellect and sound judgment, with desire and emotion following in its train.

Now the argument has come full circle, and I repeat -- whether you take it as jest or as earnest -- that the prayer of a fool is dangerous, likely to produce the opposite of what he desires. If you prefer to take my words seriously, I believe you will find, as I have said, that the ruin of the Dorian kings and their entire project was caused not by cowardice or by military ignorance on the part of either rulers or subjects, but by general moral decline -- above all, by ignorance of the most important human concerns. This was true then, is true now, and always will be true. I will try, with your permission, to demonstrate this as clearly as I can in the course of our discussion."
elegant:
The argument has come full circle. Legislation should aim at wisdom, not merely at war -- at all four virtues, led by wisdom, with desire and emotion following reason.

The prayer of a fool is dangerous, likely producing the opposite of what is desired. The Dorian kings were ruined not by cowardice or military ignorance but by moral decline and ignorance of the most important human affairs. This was true then, is true now, and always will be.
summary: The Athenian recalls his opening argument that legislation should aim at wisdom and all four virtues, not just military courage. He now applies this to the Dorian kingdoms: their ruin was caused not by cowardice or military incompetence but by general moral degeneracy and ignorance of the most important human concerns -- a universal principle that holds across all times.
distilled: The Dorian kingdoms fell not through military failure but through moral ignorance -- proving that legislation must aim at wisdom, not merely at war.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage explicitly connects Book 3's historical analysis to the argument from Book 1 (625c-632d) about whether legislation should aim at war or at complete virtue. The four virtues are the Platonic cardinal virtues: wisdom (sophia/phronesis), courage (andreia), temperance (sophrosyne), and justice (dikaiosyne) -- see Republic 427e-434d. The claim that wisdom leads with 'affection and desire in their train' articulates the psychological hierarchy of Republic 441d-442d (reason governing spirit and appetite). The phrase 'in jest if you like, or in earnest if you like' recalls Plato's concept of serious play (spoudaiogeloion), central to the Laws. The universal claim -- 'was then, is still, and always will be' -- elevates the historical lesson into a permanent truth about political life.

narrative:
"Pray go on, Stranger; compliments are troublesome, but we will show, not in word but in deed, how greatly we prize your words, for we will give them our best attention; and that is the way in which a freeman best shows his approval or disapproval."

"Excellent, Cleinias; let us do as you say."

"By all means, if Heaven wills. Go on."

"Well, then, proceeding in the same train of thought, I say that the greatest ignorance was the ruin of the Dorian power, and that now, as then, ignorance is ruin. And if this be true, the legislator must endeavour to implant wisdom in states, and banish ignorance to the utmost of his power."

"That is evident."

"Then now consider what is really the greatest ignorance. I should like to know whether you and Megillus would agree with me in what I am about to say; for my opinion is --"

"What?"
refashioned:
"Please continue, Stranger. Compliments are a burden, but we will demonstrate our esteem for your words not through flattery but through attention -- for that is how a free person best shows approval or disapproval."

"Well said, Cleinias. Let us proceed exactly that way."

"Yes, with heaven's blessing. Please go on."

"Very well. Following the same line of reasoning: the greatest ignorance destroyed the Dorian power, and ignorance remains ruinous now as it was then. If this is true, the legislator's duty is to cultivate wisdom in the state and drive out ignorance as far as humanly possible."

"That much is clear."

"Then consider: what is truly the greatest form of ignorance? I would like to know whether you and Megillus agree with what I am about to propose. My view is --"

"What is it?"
elegant:
Cleinias urges the Athenian to continue, noting that free men show esteem through attention, not flattery.

The Athenian restates the thesis: the greatest ignorance destroyed the Dorian power. Ignorance always destroys. Therefore the legislator must cultivate wisdom and banish ignorance as far as possible. But what is the greatest form of ignorance?
summary: Cleinias encourages the Athenian to continue, declaring that attentive listening is the freest form of approval. The Athenian reaffirms that ignorance destroyed the Dorian confederacy and remains the perennial cause of political ruin. The legislator's chief task is therefore to implant wisdom and banish ignorance. He prepares to define the greatest form of ignorance.
distilled: Ignorance destroyed the Dorian power and always destroys; the legislator's supreme duty is implanting wisdom and banishing ignorance.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias, Megillus, and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's remark about free men showing approval through attention rather than flattery is significant: it reflects the Laws' concern with what constitutes genuine civic freedom (as opposed to servile flattery or democratic noise). Compare Republic 563a-b on democratic flattery. Megillus's 'if Heaven wills' adds a pious Spartan note. The Athenian's thesis that ignorance is the perennial cause of political ruin sets up the forthcoming definition of 'the greatest ignorance' -- the internal conflict between pleasure and reason -- which is one of the Laws' most important philosophical contributions. The legislator-as-educator theme connects to Republic 376e-412b and the entire educational program of the Laws.

narrative:
"That the greatest ignorance is when a man hates that which he nevertheless thinks to be good and noble, and loves and embraces that which he knows to be unrighteous and evil. This disagreement between the sense of pleasure and the judgment of reason in the soul is, in my opinion, the worst ignorance; and also the greatest, because affecting the great mass of the human soul; for the principle which feels pleasure and pain in the individual is like the mass or populace in a state. And when the soul is opposed to knowledge, or opinion, or reason, which are her natural lords, that I call folly, just as in the state, when the multitude refuses to obey their rulers and the laws; or, again, in the individual, when fair reasonings have their habitation in the soul and yet do no good, but rather the reverse of good. All these cases I term the worst ignorance, whether in individuals or in states. You will understand, Stranger, that I am speaking of something which is very different from the ignorance of handicraftsmen."

"Yes, my friend, we understand and agree."
refashioned:
"The greatest ignorance is when a person hates what they know to be good and noble, yet loves and embraces what they know to be wrong and evil. This conflict between the pull of pleasure and the judgment of reason within the soul is, in my view, the worst form of ignorance -- and also the most widespread, because it affects the largest part of the human soul. The part of us that responds to pleasure and pain is like the common populace in a state. When this appetitive element rebels against knowledge, opinion, or reason -- which are its natural rulers -- I call that folly. It is exactly analogous to a state where the multitude refuses to obey their rulers and the laws. Or again, when sound arguments occupy a person's mind but produce no beneficial effect -- or even produce harm -- that too is the worst ignorance. This applies equally to individuals and to states. You understand, I trust, that I am speaking of something entirely different from mere ignorance of a trade or craft."

"Yes, my friend, we understand and agree completely."
elegant:
The greatest ignorance is the internal civil war of the soul: hating what one knows to be good while loving what one knows to be evil. This conflict between pleasure and reason is the worst and most widespread form of ignorance, because it affects the largest part of the soul -- the appetitive element, which is like the common populace in a state.

When this element rebels against reason, its natural ruler, that is folly -- just as when the masses refuse to obey rulers and laws. When sound reasoning dwells in the soul yet produces no good, that is the worst ignorance. This is entirely different from mere ignorance of a craft.
summary: The Athenian defines the greatest ignorance as the internal conflict where a person's desires rebel against their own rational judgment -- loving what they know to be evil and hating what they know to be good. He draws an explicit analogy between the soul's structure and the state: the appetitive element is like the populace, and when it refuses to obey reason (its natural ruler), the result is folly -- whether in an individual or a city.
distilled: The greatest ignorance is the soul's civil war: when pleasure rebels against reason's judgment, loving evil and hating good -- mirroring a state where the populace defies its laws.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias assents. -- This is one of the Laws' most important philosophical passages. The definition of ignorance as internal conflict between pleasure and reason directly parallels Republic 439a-441c (the tripartite soul) and the concept of akrasia (weakness of will) discussed in Protagoras 352a-358d. The soul-state analogy is the governing metaphor of the Republic (368d-369a, 441c-442d). The distinction between this 'greatest ignorance' and mere technical ignorance (ignorance of handicrafts) echoes Socrates' distinction in the Apology (22d-e) between genuine wisdom and craft-knowledge. Note that here Cleinias speaks the definition -- a dramatic touch showing that the interlocutors have internalized the Athenian's teaching method. The claim that the pleasure-seeking element is 'the great mass of the human soul' is psychologically astute and grounds the political argument: just as appetite dominates the individual soul, the masses dominate the state.

narrative: "Let us, then, in the first place declare and affirm that the citizen who does not know these things ought never to have any kind of authority entrusted to him: he must be stigmatized as ignorant, even though he be versed in calculation and skilled in all sorts of accomplishments, and feats of mental dexterity; and the opposite are to be called wise, even although, in the words of the proverb, they know neither how to read nor how to swim; and to them, as to men of sense, authority is to be committed. For, O my friends, how can there be the least shadow of wisdom when there is no harmony? There is none; but the noblest and greatest of harmonies may be truly said to be the greatest wisdom; and of this he is a partaker who lives according to reason; whereas he who is devoid of reason is the destroyer of his house and the very opposite of a saviour of the state: he is utterly ignorant of political wisdom. Let this, then, as I was saying, be laid down by us."
refashioned:
"Let us therefore establish this principle: a citizen who lacks this understanding should never be entrusted with any form of authority. He must be classified as ignorant, even if he is a mathematical genius, skilled in every accomplishment, and capable of extraordinary intellectual feats. Conversely, those who possess inner harmony between desire and reason should be called wise, even if -- as the old saying goes -- they can neither read nor swim. Authority should be given to them, as people of genuine understanding.

For, my friends, how can there be even a trace of wisdom without inner harmony? There cannot. The noblest and greatest of all harmonies is rightly called the greatest wisdom. The person who lives according to reason participates in this harmony. The person who lacks reason is the destroyer of his household and the very opposite of a savior of the state -- utterly devoid of political wisdom. Let us establish this as our foundational principle."
elegant:
No citizen without inner harmony between desire and reason should ever hold authority -- regardless of intellectual accomplishments. Conversely, those with such harmony deserve to be called wise, even if they lack formal education.

True wisdom is the greatest harmony: living according to reason. Without it, a person is the destroyer of his household and the opposite of a statesman. Let this be our foundational principle.
summary: The Athenian establishes the principle that authority should be entrusted only to those who possess inner harmony between desire and reason -- true political wisdom. Intellectual accomplishments without this harmony are worthless for governance, while genuine alignment of desire with reason constitutes wisdom even without formal learning. He defines true wisdom as the greatest harmony: living according to reason.
distilled: True political wisdom is inner harmony between desire and reason, and only those who possess it should hold authority -- regardless of other intellectual accomplishments.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The proverb 'neither how to read nor how to swim' was a Greek expression for complete ignorance, used here paradoxically: those with moral wisdom outrank those with mere technical knowledge. This radically redefines political competence. The identification of wisdom with harmony (harmonia) draws on Pythagorean and musical traditions -- see Republic 430e-432a on temperance as harmony. The phrase 'destroyer of his house' versus 'saviour of the state' creates a stark binary that echoes the Homeric epithet soter (savior) applied to Zeus and political leaders. Compare Statesman 293d-e on the true statesman possessing knowledge. The principle that intellectual brilliance without moral wisdom disqualifies one from office challenges meritocratic assumptions based on technical competence.

narrative:
"Let it be so laid down."

"I suppose that there must be rulers and subjects in states?"

"Certainly."

"And what are the principles on which men rule and obey in cities, whether great or small; and similarly in families? What are they, and how many in number? Is there not one claim of authority which is always just -- that of fathers and mothers and in general of progenitors to rule over their offspring?"

"There is."

"Next follows the principle that the noble should rule over the ignoble; and, thirdly, that the elder should rule and the younger obey?"

"To be sure."

"And, fourthly, that slaves should be ruled, and their masters rule?"

"Of course."

"Fifthly, if I am not mistaken, comes the principle that the stronger shall rule, and the weaker be ruled?"

"That is a rule not to be disobeyed."
refashioned:
"Let that be established."

"Now, there must necessarily be rulers and subjects in every state?"

"Of course."

"What principles determine who rules and who obeys, in cities large and small, and in families as well? How many such principles are there? First, there is one claim to authority that is always just: the right of parents -- fathers and mothers and ancestors generally -- to rule over their children."

"That is so."

"Second comes the principle that the well-born should rule over the common. Third, that the elder should rule and the younger obey."

"Certainly."

"Fourth, the principle that slaves should be ruled and their masters should rule."

"Of course."

"And fifth, if I am not mistaken, the principle that the stronger shall rule and the weaker be ruled."

"That is a principle not easily defied."
elegant: Every state requires rulers and subjects. The principles of authority are: first, the always-just right of parents over children; second, the noble over the common; third, the elder over the younger; fourth, the master over the slave; fifth, the stronger over the weaker.
summary: The Athenian begins cataloging the principles that determine who rules and who obeys. He identifies five claims to authority: parental right over children (always just), nobility over commonness, age over youth, master over slave, and strength over weakness.
distilled: Five principles of authority govern political life: parentage, nobility, seniority, mastery, and strength.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This enumeration of claims to political authority is one of Plato's most systematic treatments of the question 'who should rule?' It parallels and extends Aristotle's analysis in Politics 1284a-b. The five principles listed so far represent different traditional justifications: (1) natural/familial, (2) aristocratic, (3) gerontocratic, (4) institutional/servile, (5) might-makes-right. The Athenian notably calls only parental authority 'always just,' leaving the others implicitly qualified. The principle of the stronger ruling is attributed to nature and acknowledged as practically inescapable, but the Athenian will soon subordinate it to the sixth principle (wisdom). Compare Gorgias 483d-484a (Callicles' argument that the stronger should rule by nature) and Republic 338c (Thrasymachus's 'justice is the interest of the stronger').

narrative:
"Yes, and a rule which prevails very widely among all creatures, and is according to nature, as the Theban poet Pindar once said; and the sixth principle, and the greatest of all, is, that the wise should lead and command, and the ignorant follow and obey; and yet, O thou most wise Pindar, as I should reply him, this surely is not contrary to nature, but according to nature, being the rule of law over willing subjects, and not a rule of compulsion."

"Most true."

"There is a seventh kind of rule which is awarded by lot, and is dear to the Gods and a token of good fortune: he on whom the lot falls is a ruler, and he who fails in obtaining the lot goes away and is the subject; and this we affirm to be quite just."

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"Yes -- and this principle of strength prevails widely among all living creatures and is 'according to nature,' as the Theban poet Pindar once declared. But the sixth principle, and the greatest of all, is that the wise should lead and command while the ignorant follow and obey. And yet, O most wise Pindar, I would reply to you that this rule of wisdom is not contrary to nature but is the truest expression of nature -- it is the rule of law over willing subjects, not rule by force."

"That is absolutely right."

"There is also a seventh principle of rule: selection by lot, which is dear to the gods and considered a mark of divine favor. Whoever the lot selects becomes the ruler; whoever it passes over becomes the subject. We declare this too to be just."

"Indeed."
elegant:
The rule of the stronger prevails widely in nature, as Pindar declared. But the sixth and greatest principle is that the wise should rule the ignorant. This, contrary to what Pindar may imply, is not opposed to nature but is its truest expression -- law ruling willing subjects, not compulsion.

The seventh principle is selection by lot: dear to the gods, a token of fortune, and considered just.
summary: The Athenian completes his catalog of authority: the fifth principle (rule of the strong) is widespread in nature, as Pindar noted. But the sixth and greatest is that the wise should rule the ignorant -- not through force but through law over willing subjects. The seventh is selection by lot, considered divinely sanctioned and just.
distilled: The greatest principle of authority is that the wise should rule the ignorant through law and consent -- surpassing even the natural rule of the strong.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The reference is to Pindar's famous fragment 169a ('Law [Nomos] is the king of all, mortals and immortals alike... making just the most violent deed with a sovereign hand'), one of the most debated passages in Greek literature. Plato's clever response redefines 'according to nature': the rule of wisdom over willing subjects is more natural than brute force. This directly counters Callicles in Gorgias 484b, who used the same Pindar fragment to justify the strong dominating the weak. The seven principles of authority form a hierarchy culminating in wisdom -- the same hierarchy as the Republic's philosophical kingship (473c-d). The seventh principle (lot) reflects Athenian democratic practice where magistrates were often chosen by lot (sortition). By calling it 'dear to the Gods,' Plato gives qualified approval while keeping it subordinate to wisdom. Compare Statesman 291d-292a on the classification of constitutions.

narrative: "Then now," as we say playfully to any of those who lightly undertake the making of laws, "you see, legislator, the principles of government, how many they are, and that they are naturally opposed to each other. There we have discovered a fountain-head of seditions, to which you must attend. And, first, we will ask you to consider with us, how and in what respect the kings of Argos and Messene violated these our maxims, and ruined themselves and the great and famous Hellenic power of the olden time. Was it because they did not know how wisely Hesiod spoke when he said that the half is often more than the whole? His meaning was, that when to take the whole would be dangerous, and to take the half would be the safe and moderate course, then the moderate or better was more than the immoderate or worse."
refashioned: "Now then," we might say half-playfully to anyone who casually takes up the work of legislation, "you see, legislator, how many principles of government there are, and how they naturally conflict with one another. Here we have found the very source of political strife, which demands your attention. First, let us consider how and in what way the kings of Argos and Messene violated these principles and destroyed both themselves and the great Hellenic power of ancient times. Was it because they failed to heed Hesiod's wise saying that 'the half is often more than the whole'? What Hesiod meant was this: when grasping for everything is dangerous and taking half would be the safe and moderate path, then moderation is worth more than excess -- the better course outweighs the worse."
elegant:
The seven principles of government naturally conflict with each other -- and here is the very source of political sedition. The legislator must understand this.

The kings of Argos and Messene failed because they ignored Hesiod's maxim: 'the half is often more than the whole.' When taking everything is dangerous and moderation is safe, the moderate path is worth more than the excessive one.
summary: The Athenian warns that the seven competing principles of authority naturally conflict, creating the perpetual source of political sedition. He traces the ruin of Argos and Messene to their kings' failure to understand Hesiod's principle of moderation: 'the half is often more than the whole' -- meaning that when grasping for total power is dangerous, taking less is actually gaining more.
distilled: Competing principles of authority are the source of all political sedition, and kings who grasp for total power rather than practicing Hesiod's moderation destroy themselves.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- Hesiod's maxim 'the half is more than the whole' comes from Works and Days 40 and was one of the most famous lines in Greek wisdom literature. The Athenian reinterprets it as a principle of political moderation: rulers who limit their own power actually gain more than those who seize everything. This is the core lesson of the Dorian failure. The metaphor of a 'fountain-head of seditions' (stasis) identifies the fundamental problem of constitutional design: the competing claims to authority must be balanced, not resolved in favor of any single principle. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1301a-1302b on the causes of revolution (stasis). The playful address to the hypothetical legislator maintains the Laws' characteristic tone of serious play (spoudaiogeloion). The principle of moderation connects to Aristotle's later concept of the 'mean' (mesotes) and to the entire Greek tradition of meden agan ('nothing in excess').

narrative:
"Very true."

"And may we suppose this immoderate spirit to be more fatal when found among kings than when among peoples?"

"The probability is that ignorance will be a disorder especially prevalent among kings, because they lead a proud and luxurious life."

"Is it not palpable that the chief aim of the kings of that time was to get the better of the established laws, and that they were not in harmony with the principles which they had agreed to observe by word and oath? This want of harmony may have had the appearance of wisdom, but was really, as we assert, the greatest ignorance, and utterly overthrew the whole empire by dissonance and harsh discord."

"Very likely."

"Good; and what measures ought the legislator to have then taken in order to avert this calamity? Truly there is no great wisdom in knowing, and no great difficulty in telling, after the evil has happened; but to have foreseen the remedy at the time would have taken a much wiser head than ours."

"What do you mean?"

"Any one who looks at what has occurred with you Lacedaemonians, Megillus, may easily know and may easily say what ought to have been done at that time."

"Speak a little more clearly."

"Nothing can be clearer than the observation which I am about to make."

"What is it?"
refashioned:
"Indeed."

"And would not this immoderate spirit be even more dangerous in kings than in ordinary people?"

"It is likely that this kind of ignorance is especially common among kings, since they lead lives of pride and luxury."

"Is it not obvious that the kings of that era aimed above all to override the established laws -- that they were not in harmony with the principles they had sworn oaths to uphold? This disharmony may have looked like cunning wisdom, but it was actually the greatest ignorance, and it destroyed the entire empire through its discord."

"That seems very probable."

"Now, what should the legislator have done to prevent this catastrophe? Of course, it takes no great wisdom to diagnose a problem after it has happened, and no great difficulty to prescribe a remedy in hindsight. Foreseeing the solution at the time would have required far greater wisdom than ours."

"What are you suggesting?"

"Simply this: anyone who examines what happened in Sparta, Megillus, can easily determine what should have been done then."

"Be more specific."

"Nothing could be clearer than what I am about to say."

"Go on."
elegant:
Immoderate ambition is most dangerous in kings, whose proud and luxurious lives breed the greatest ignorance. The Dorian kings aimed to override the very laws they had sworn to uphold -- a disharmony that looked like cleverness but was in fact the worst ignorance, destroying the empire through discord.

Hindsight requires no wisdom. The real question is what the legislator should have done to prevent this -- and Sparta's example provides the answer.
summary: The Athenian argues that the immoderate spirit of the Dorian kings -- their drive to override established laws despite sworn oaths -- was especially dangerous because luxury and pride made royalty uniquely prone to this form of ignorance. While diagnosing the problem in hindsight is easy, he suggests that Sparta's constitutional history reveals the preventive measures that should have been taken.
distilled: Kings are most susceptible to the greatest ignorance -- mistaking lawlessness for wisdom -- and Sparta's constitutional evolution reveals the remedy hindsight makes obvious.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The observation that kings are especially prone to ignorance because of luxury connects to the Persian narrative that follows (3.37-3.41). The distinction between hindsight wisdom and foresight reflects a genuine philosophical problem about political knowledge -- see Statesman 294a-b on the difficulty of legislating for unforeseen circumstances. The Spartan constitutional example the Athenian is about to describe (dual kingship, the Gerousia, the Ephorate) represents Plato's most sustained praise of an actual existing constitution. The phrase 'dissonance and harsh discord' continues the musical metaphor for political harmony that runs through the Laws (cf. Book 2's discussion of music and education).

narrative:
"That if any one gives too great a power to anything, too large a sail to a vessel, too much food to the body, too much authority to the mind, and does not observe the mean, everything is overthrown, and, in the wantonness of excess runs in the one case to disorders, and in the other to injustice, which is the child of excess. I mean to say, my dear friends, that there is no soul of man, young and irresponsible, who will be able to sustain the temptation of arbitrary power -- no one who will not, under such circumstances, become filled with folly, that worst of diseases, and be hated by his nearest and dearest friends: when this happens, his kingdom is undermined, and all his power vanishes from him. And great legislators who know the mean should take heed of the danger. As far as we can guess at this distance of time, what happened was as follows --"

"What?"
refashioned:
"If you give too much power to anything -- too large a sail to a ship, too much food to the body, too much authority to the mind -- and fail to observe the mean, everything is thrown into chaos. Excess produces physical disorders in one case and injustice in the other, for injustice is the offspring of excess. Let me state this plainly, my friends: no human soul, especially a young and untested one, can withstand the temptation of absolute power. Without exception, such power fills a person with folly -- the worst of all diseases -- until they are hated by their closest friends and allies. When that happens, the kingdom collapses and all power evaporates. Legislators who understand the principle of the mean must guard against this danger. Based on what we can reconstruct from this distance in time, here is what happened --"

"Tell us."
elegant:
Excess destroys everything: too large a sail capsizes a ship, too much food sickens the body, too much authority corrupts the mind. Injustice is the child of excess.

No human soul -- especially a young one -- can withstand unlimited power without being filled with folly. The result is universal hatred, collapsed authority, and vanished power. Wise legislators must observe the mean.
summary: The Athenian articulates his central principle: excess in any domain leads to destruction, whether it is a sail too large for a ship or authority too great for a mind. No human soul can resist the corruption of unlimited power, which inevitably produces folly, alienation, and the collapse of authority. Great legislators must therefore design constitutions around the principle of the mean.
distilled: No human soul can withstand unlimited power without becoming corrupt; excess always produces its own destruction, and wise constitutions must observe the mean.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Megillus responds. -- The sailing metaphor is apt: Greek political vocabulary frequently drew on nautical imagery (kubernetes = pilot/governor). The claim that 'no soul of man' can withstand arbitrary power is one of Plato's most famous assertions about human nature -- it is the philosophical foundation for constitutional government over personal rule. Compare Republic 360b-c (the Ring of Gyges: would anyone resist corruption with absolute power?) and Lord Acton's 'power tends to corrupt.' The identification of folly as 'the worst of diseases' echoes 3.26's definition of the greatest ignorance. The principle of the mean (to meson/to metrion) here anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean in Nicomachean Ethics 1106b-1107a, though Plato applies it institutionally rather than individually.

narrative:
"A God, who watched over Sparta, seeing into the future, gave you two families of kings instead of one; and thus brought you more within the limits of moderation. In the next place, some human wisdom mingled with divine power, observing that the constitution of your government was still feverish and excited, tempered your inborn strength and pride of birth with the moderation which comes of age, making the power of your twenty-eight elders equal with that of the kings in the most important matters. But your third saviour, perceiving that your government was still swelling and foaming, and desirous to impose a curb upon it, instituted the Ephors, whose power he made to resemble that of magistrates elected by lot; and by this arrangement the kingly office, being compounded of the right elements and duly moderated, was preserved, and was the means of preserving all the rest.

Since, if there had been only the original legislators, Temenus, Cresphontes, and their contemporaries, as far as they were concerned not even the portion of Aristodemus would have been preserved; for they had no proper experience in legislation, or they would surely not have imagined that oaths would moderate a youthful spirit invested with a power which might be converted into a tyranny. Now that God has instructed us what sort of government would have been or will be lasting, there is no wisdom, as I have already said, in judging after the event; there is no difficulty in learning from an example which has already occurred. But if any one could have foreseen all this at the time, and had been able to moderate the government of the three kingdoms and unite them into one, he might have saved all the excellent institutions which were then conceived; and no Persian or any other armament would have dared to attack us, or would have regarded Hellas as a power to be despised."

"True."
refashioned:
"A god watching over Sparta, foreseeing the future, gave you two royal families instead of one -- thus bringing the monarchy closer to moderation. Then a combination of human wisdom and divine guidance, recognizing that the constitution was still dangerously overheated, tempered the kings' inborn strength and aristocratic pride with the restraining influence of age: the council of twenty-eight elders was given power equal to the kings' on the most critical matters. But a third preserver, seeing that the government was still surging and overflowing, imposed yet another check -- the institution of the Ephors, whose power resembled that of magistrates chosen by lot. Through this arrangement, the kingship was composed of properly balanced elements and duly moderated, and its preservation in turn preserved everything else.

Had there been only the original legislators -- Temenus, Cresphontes, and their contemporaries -- not even Sparta's own share would have survived. They lacked real legislative experience, or they would never have imagined that mere oaths could restrain a young spirit armed with power that could easily become tyrannical. Now that events have shown us what kind of government endures, there is -- as I have said -- no special wisdom in judging after the fact. It is easy to learn from what has already happened. But if someone at the time had possessed the foresight to moderate and unite all three kingdoms, he might have preserved every excellent institution they had conceived. No Persian or any other military power would have dared to attack Greece or would have treated it as a power to be despised."

"That is true."
elegant:
Sparta was saved by three successive checks on royal power. First, a god gave two royal families instead of one. Second, human wisdom created the council of twenty-eight elders with power equal to the kings'. Third, the Ephors were instituted as a further restraint, resembling magistrates chosen by lot. Each check moderated the constitution further, and the balanced kingship preserved everything.

The original Dorian legislators naively trusted oaths to restrain young men armed with potentially tyrannical power. Had someone foreseen the need for institutional moderation and united all three kingdoms, Greece would have been an invincible power that no Persian army would have dared to challenge.
summary: The Athenian describes Sparta's three-stage constitutional moderation: dual kingship by divine providence, the Gerousia (council of twenty-eight elders) by human-divine wisdom, and the Ephorate as a final democratic check. Each institution further restrained royal power. He contrasts this with the original Dorian legislators' naive reliance on oaths, arguing that had all three kingdoms adopted such institutional checks and remained united, Greece would have been invincible against Persia.
distilled: Sparta survived because divine and human wisdom progressively checked royal power through dual kingship, the Gerousia, and the Ephorate -- institutional moderation that oaths alone could never achieve.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias assents. -- This is the Laws' most detailed analysis of the Spartan constitution. The three 'saviours' correspond to: (1) the mythical division into two royal houses (Agiads and Eurypontids), (2) Lycurgus's institution of the Gerousia (council of 28 elders plus 2 kings = 30), and (3) the later institution of the Ephorate (traditionally attributed to Theopompus, 8th century BCE, or Chilon, 6th century). The metaphor of 'feverish,' 'swelling and foaming' for unchecked government is medical-political, connecting to the physician analogy from 3.17. The claim that oaths cannot restrain 'a youthful spirit invested with power' restates 3.32's principle that no soul withstands unlimited power. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1265b and 1313a on the Spartan constitution. The counterfactual about united Greek power against Persia connects to the actual Persian Wars narrative in 3.45-3.46.

narrative:
"There was small credit to us, Cleinias, in defeating them; and the discredit was, not that the conquerors did not win glorious victories both by land and sea, but what, in my opinion, brought discredit was, first of all, the circumstance that of the three cities one only fought on behalf of Hellas, and the two others were so utterly good for nothing that the one was waging a mighty war against Lacedaemon, and was thus preventing her from rendering assistance, while the city of Argos, which had the precedence at the time of the distribution, when asked to aid in repelling the barbarian, would not answer to the call, or give aid.

Many things might be told about Hellas in connection with that war which are far from honourable; nor, indeed, can we rightly say that Hellas repelled the invader; for the truth is, that unless the Athenians and Lacedaemonians, acting in concert, had warded off the impending yoke, all the tribes of Hellas would have been fused in a chaos of Hellenes mingling with one another, of barbarians mingling with Hellenes, and Hellenes with barbarians; just as nations who are now subject to the Persian power, owing to unnatural separations and combinations of them, are dispersed and scattered, and live miserably.

These, Cleinias and Megillus, are the reproaches which we have to make against statesmen and legislators, as they are called, past and present, if we would analyse the causes of their failure, and find out what else might have been done. We said, for instance, just now, that there ought to be no great and unmixed powers; and this was under the idea that a state ought to be free and wise and harmonious, and that a legislator ought to legislate with a view to this end. Nor is there any reason to be surprised at our continually proposing aims for the legislator which appear not to be always the same; but we should consider when we say that temperance is to be the aim, or wisdom is to be the aim, or friendship is to be the aim, that all these aims are really the same; and if so, a variety in the modes of expression ought not to disturb us."
refashioned:
"There is little credit to Greece, Cleinias, in defeating the Persians. The disgrace was not that the victors failed to win glorious battles by land and sea -- they certainly did. The real disgrace was that of the three Dorian kingdoms, only one fought for Greece. Messene was waging a major war against Sparta, preventing it from offering full assistance. And Argos -- which held the position of precedence from the original settlement -- simply refused to answer the call when asked to help repel the barbarian.

Much could be said about Greece's conduct in that war that is far from honorable. In truth, we cannot properly say that 'Greece' repelled the invader at all. Had Athens and Sparta not acted together, every Greek people would have been thrown into a chaotic mixture -- Greeks jumbled together with Greeks, barbarians with Greeks, Greeks with barbarians -- just as the nations now under Persian control have been unnaturally divided and combined, scattered and dispersed, living in misery.

These are the charges we must bring against statesmen and legislators, past and present, if we want to understand why they failed and what should have been done differently. Our key principle has been that there should be no great, unchecked powers -- because a state should be free, wise, and harmonious, and legislation should aim at producing this condition. We should not be confused by the fact that we keep proposing different goals for the legislator -- temperance, wisdom, friendship. These are all expressions of the same fundamental aim, and the variety of language should not trouble us."
elegant:
Greece deserves little credit for defeating Persia. Only one of three Dorian kingdoms fought; Messene was at war with Sparta, and Argos refused to help. Had Athens and Sparta not allied, all Greeks would have been scrambled into a chaotic fusion with barbarians -- as happened to the nations now under Persian rule.

The lesson for legislators: there must be no great unchecked powers. A state must be free, wise, and harmonious. When we variously name the aim as temperance, wisdom, or friendship, these are all the same fundamental goal.
summary: The Athenian delivers a sobering assessment of the Persian Wars: Greece barely survived because only Sparta and Athens fought, while Messene and Argos failed to contribute. Without that alliance, Greek civilization would have been destroyed. He draws the legislative lesson that unchecked power must be avoided, and that temperance, wisdom, and friendship are merely different expressions of the same political goal: freedom, wisdom, and harmony.
distilled: Greece's narrow survival against Persia -- saved only by the Athens-Sparta alliance -- proves that unchecked power destroys states, and that temperance, wisdom, and friendship are one legislative aim.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is a remarkable passage for an Athenian character to deliver: it credits Athens alongside Sparta while sharply criticizing the broader Greek failure. The mention of Argos's refusal echoes Herodotus 7.148-152. The Messenian War that distracted Sparta is the Third Messenian War (revolt of helots, c. 464 BCE), though the chronology is somewhat compressed. The nightmarish image of Greeks and barbarians 'fused in chaos' expresses a deep Greek cultural anxiety about loss of identity. The identification of temperance, wisdom, and friendship as different names for the same aim is a key philosophical move: it asserts the unity of the virtues (a Socratic thesis from Protagoras 329c-333b) at the political level. The principle against 'great and unmixed powers' is the Laws' central constitutional doctrine, anticipating Montesquieu's separation of powers and Madison's checks and balances in Federalist No. 51.

narrative:
"Let us resume the argument in that spirit. And now, speaking of friendship and wisdom and freedom, I wish that you would tell me at what, in your opinion, the legislator should aim."

"Hear me, then: there are two mother forms of states from which the rest may be truly said to be derived; and one of them may be called monarchy and the other democracy: the Persians have the highest form of the one, and we of the other; almost all the rest, as I was saying, are variations of these. Now, if you are to have liberty and the combination of friendship with wisdom, you must have both these forms of government in a measure; the argument emphatically declares that no city can be well governed which is not made up of both."
refashioned:
"Let us continue in that spirit. Now, regarding friendship, wisdom, and freedom -- I would like you to tell me what you think the legislator's ultimate aim should be."

"Listen carefully: there are two fundamental forms of government from which all others derive. One may be called monarchy, the other democracy. Persia represents the fullest expression of monarchy; Athens represents the fullest expression of democracy. Nearly all other constitutions are variations of these two. Now, if you want to achieve liberty combined with friendship and wisdom, you must incorporate elements of both forms in measured proportions. Our argument makes one thing absolutely clear: no city can be well governed that does not blend both monarchy and democracy."
elegant:
All constitutions derive from two mother forms: monarchy (exemplified by Persia) and democracy (exemplified by Athens). Most other systems are variations of these two.

To achieve liberty, friendship, and wisdom, a state must blend both forms. No city that relies exclusively on one can be well governed.
summary: The Athenian identifies monarchy and democracy as the two fundamental forms of government from which all others derive, with Persia and Athens as their respective exemplars. He argues that a well-governed city must combine elements of both, since neither pure monarchy nor pure democracy alone can produce the combination of liberty, friendship, and wisdom that good government requires.
distilled: All constitutions derive from two mother forms -- monarchy and democracy -- and good government requires a measured blend of both.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias asks; the Athenian Stranger responds. -- This is one of the most influential passages in the history of political theory. The 'mixed constitution' thesis -- that good government blends monarchical and democratic elements -- was adopted by Aristotle (Politics 1293b-1294a), Polybius (Histories 6.3-18, applied to the Roman Republic), Cicero (De Re Publica 1.45), and Montesquieu. The choice of Persia and Athens as polar examples is deliberate: the entire subsequent analysis (3.37-3.49) will show how each failed by excess. The phrase 'mother forms' (meteres politeion) suggests these are generative principles rather than merely descriptive categories. Compare Republic 544c-545a, which classifies five regime types in a different framework. The Laws' mixed constitution model represents Plato's mature departure from the Republic's philosopher-king ideal.

narrative:
"Impossible."

"Neither the one, if it be exclusively and excessively attached to monarchy, nor the other, if it be similarly attached to freedom, observes moderation; but your states, the Laconian and Cretan, have more of it; and the same was the case with the Athenians and Persians of old time, but now they have less. Shall I tell you why?"

"By all means, if it will tend to elucidate our subject."
refashioned:
"Exactly -- it cannot be done otherwise."

"A state exclusively and excessively committed to monarchy cannot achieve moderation, nor can one similarly devoted to unlimited freedom. Your states, however -- Sparta and Crete -- possess more of this balance. Athens and Persia once had it too, but both have lost ground. Shall I explain why?"

"Please do, if it will help clarify our subject."
elegant: Neither pure monarchy nor pure democracy achieves moderation. Sparta and Crete possess more balance. Athens and Persia once had it but have since declined. The question is why.
summary: The Athenian observes that neither exclusive monarchy nor exclusive democracy achieves moderation. Sparta and Crete currently possess the best balance, while Athens and Persia -- which once had it -- have since declined. He prepares to explain the causes of this deterioration.
distilled: Sparta and Crete achieve the constitutional balance that Athens and Persia have lost through their respective excesses of democracy and monarchy.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This passage sets up the extended parallel analysis of Persian and Athenian decline (3.37-3.49). The compliment to Spartan and Cretan moderation is diplomatic, flattering both interlocutors, but also substantive: Plato genuinely admired aspects of both constitutions. The claim that Athens once had moderation but has lost it is historically specific -- the Athenian is about to describe the pre-Persian War constitution as more balanced (3.45-3.46). The decline of both Persia and Athens serves as a double cautionary tale about the dangers of excess in either direction. Compare Aristotle's Politics 1296a on the middle constitution as the best, and Polybius 6.10 on the Roman mixed constitution as superior to purely monarchical or democratic forms.

narrative: "Hear, then: There was a time when the Persians had more of the state which is a mean between slavery and freedom. In the reign of Cyrus they were freemen and also lords of many others: the rulers gave a share of freedom to the subjects, and being treated as equals, the soldiers were on better terms with their generals, and showed themselves more ready in the hour of danger. And if there was any wise man among them, who was able to give good counsel, he imparted his wisdom to the public; for the king was not jealous, but allowed him full liberty of speech, and gave honour to those who could advise him in any matter. And the nation waxed in all respects, because there was freedom and friendship and communion of mind among them."
refashioned: "Listen, then. There was a time when the Persian state achieved a genuine balance between servitude and freedom. Under Cyrus, the Persians were free men who also ruled over many others. The rulers shared a degree of freedom with their subjects, treating soldiers as equals, which produced better relations between troops and commanders and greater willingness to face danger. If any wise person among them had good advice to offer, they could share it publicly, because the king was not jealous -- he permitted full freedom of speech and honored those who could counsel him well. The entire nation flourished because freedom, friendship, and shared purpose prevailed among them."
elegant: Under Cyrus, Persia achieved the ideal balance between authority and freedom. Rulers shared freedom with subjects, soldiers were treated as equals, and the king welcomed wise counsel without jealousy. The nation flourished because freedom, friendship, and communion of mind prevailed.
summary: The Athenian describes Cyrus's Persia as a model of balanced government: rulers shared freedom with subjects, military discipline was built on equality rather than fear, and the king freely welcomed wise counsel without jealousy. This combination of freedom, friendship, and shared purpose produced national flourishing.
distilled: Cyrus's Persia flourished because it balanced authority with freedom, treating subjects as equals and welcoming wise counsel without royal jealousy.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This idealized portrait of Cyrus's Persia draws on Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Herodotus 1.125-130. The three qualities identified -- freedom (eleutheria), friendship (philia), and 'communion of mind' (koinonia nou) -- are precisely the goals the Athenian set for legislation in 3.34-3.35. The emphasis on parrhesia (freedom of speech) as essential to good governance is remarkable given Plato's reservations about democratic free speech elsewhere. The portrait implicitly contrasts with contemporary Persia under Artaxerxes, which had become despotic. The claim that treating soldiers as equals produced better military performance reflects actual military sociology -- contrast the Persian army's later reliance on mercenaries (3.44). Compare Xenophon's Cyropaedia 1.2.1-8 on Persian education and equality.

narrative:
"That certainly appears to have been the case."

"How, then, was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and again recovered under Darius? Shall I try to divine?"

"The enquiry, no doubt, has a bearing upon our subject."

"I imagine that Cyrus, though a great and patriotic general, had never given his mind to education, and never attended to the order of his household."

"What makes you say so?"
refashioned:
"That certainly seems to have been the case."

"Then how was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and how was it later recovered under Darius? Shall I attempt an explanation?"

"The question clearly bears on our subject."

"My guess is that Cyrus, for all his greatness as a patriotic military leader, never turned his attention to education, and never properly managed his own household."

"What leads you to that conclusion?"
elegant: How was Persia's balanced constitution lost under Cambyses and recovered under Darius? The answer lies in Cyrus's great blind spot: though a brilliant general, he never attended to education or the proper ordering of his household.
summary: The Athenian asks how Persia lost its constitutional balance under Cambyses and recovered it under Darius. He proposes that Cyrus, despite being a great military leader, fatally neglected education and the management of his own household -- setting the stage for his sons' corruption.
distilled: Cyrus's fatal flaw was neglecting education and household order despite his military greatness, leading to Persia's constitutional decline under his successor.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The sequence of Persian kings -- Cyrus, Cambyses, Darius -- follows Herodotus's narrative (Books 1-3). The critique of Cyrus as militarily great but educationally negligent is a deliberate provocation, given that Xenophon's Cyropaedia presents Cyrus as an ideal of education. The emphasis on household management (oikonomia) as a prerequisite for political leadership connects to the Greek analogy between household and state governance -- see Xenophon's Oeconomicus and Aristotle's Politics 1253b. The Athenian's claim to 'divine' rather than know echoes Socratic language about likely stories (eikos mythos) from the Timaeus. The question of how good constitutions degenerate is central to Republic 545d-576b.

narrative:
"I think that from his youth upwards he was a soldier, and entrusted the education of his children to the women; and they brought them up from their childhood as the favourites of fortune, who were blessed already, and needed no more blessings. They thought that they were happy enough, and that no one should be allowed to oppose them in any way, and they compelled every one to praise all that they said or did. This was how they brought them up."

"A splendid education truly!"

"Such an one as women were likely to give them, and especially princesses who had recently grown rich, and in the absence of the men, too, who were occupied in wars and dangers, and had no time to look after them."

"What would you expect?"
refashioned:
"I believe that Cyrus was a soldier from youth onwards and left his children's education entirely to the women. They raised the royal children as fortune's favorites -- already blessed, needing nothing more. They were taught that they were supremely happy, that no one should ever oppose them, and that everyone must praise whatever they said or did. That was their upbringing."

"What a magnificent education indeed!"

"Exactly the kind of education you would expect from women -- especially princesses who had recently become wealthy -- raising children in the absence of the men, who were away at war and had no time to supervise."

"What else could you expect?"
elegant: Cyrus the soldier left his children's education to the palace women, who raised them as fortune's favorites: already blessed, needing nothing, never to be opposed, always to be praised. This was the education of princesses who had recently grown wealthy, raising children without fathers who were perpetually at war.
summary: The Athenian explains that Cyrus, being a lifelong soldier, delegated his children's education to the palace women. They raised the princes as spoiled favorites of fortune -- never opposed, always flattered -- the predictable result of wealthy princesses raising children in the absence of fathers consumed by war.
distilled: Cyrus's sons were ruined by an upbringing of universal flattery and zero opposition, entrusted to wealthy palace women while their father was perpetually at war.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with ironic interjections. -- The sarcastic 'splendid education truly!' is rare in Plato's dialogues and gives the passage a sharp, conversational quality. The critique of women's education is characteristically Platonic in its complexity: while seemingly misogynistic by modern standards, the actual blame falls on the structural conditions (absent fathers, sudden wealth, court flattery) rather than on women per se. Compare Republic 451c-457b, where Plato argues for equal education of women -- a radical position. The portrait of royal children raised in flattery directly anticipates the Persian analysis in 3.40-3.41 and connects to the Laws' broader argument that unearned privilege corrupts. The theme of education as the foundation of political stability is central to both the Republic and the Laws.

narrative:
"Their father had possessions of cattle and sheep, and many herds of men and other animals, but he did not consider that those to whom he was about to make them over were not trained in his own calling, which was Persian; for the Persians are shepherds -- sons of a rugged land, which is a stern mother, and well fitted to produce sturdy race able to live in the open air and go without sleep, and also to fight, if fighting is required. He did not observe that his sons were trained differently; through the so-called blessing of being royal they were educated in the Median fashion by women and eunuchs, which led to their becoming such as people do become when they are brought up unreproved.

And so, after the death of Cyrus, his sons, in the fulness of luxury and licence, took the kingdom, and first one slew the other because he could not endure a rival; and, afterwards, the slayer himself, mad with wine and brutality, lost his kingdom through the Medes and the Eunuch, as they called him, who despised the folly of Cambyses."

"So runs the tale, and such probably were the facts."

"Yes; and the tradition says, that the empire came back to the Persians, through Darius and the seven chiefs."

"True."
refashioned:
"Cyrus possessed vast flocks of cattle and sheep, and governed great multitudes of people and other resources. But he failed to consider that his heirs were not trained in his own way of life -- the Persian way. The Persians are a shepherd people, sons of a harsh and unforgiving land, a stern mother that produces a hardy race capable of living outdoors, going without sleep, and fighting when necessary. Cyrus did not notice that his sons were being raised in a completely different manner. Under the supposed privilege of royalty, they received a Median education from women and eunuchs, which made them exactly what people always become when they grow up without discipline or correction.

After Cyrus died, his sons inherited the kingdom amid luxury and license. One brother killed the other, unable to tolerate a rival. Then the killer himself -- Cambyses, driven mad by drink and brutality -- lost the kingdom to the Medes and the man they called 'the Eunuch,' who held Cambyses' folly in contempt."

"So the story goes, and it was probably true."

"Yes, and tradition tells us the empire was restored to the Persians through Darius and the seven conspirators."

"Correct."
elegant:
Cyrus failed to see that his heirs were not raised as Persians -- a shepherd people from harsh land, bred for endurance and combat. Instead, his sons received a Median court education from women and eunuchs, becoming what people always become when raised without correction.

The result: after Cyrus's death, one son killed the other out of jealousy, and the murderer Cambyses, mad with drink and violence, lost the empire to those who despised his folly. The empire was later restored through Darius and his seven co-conspirators.
summary: The Athenian contrasts the hardy Persian shepherd culture that produced Cyrus with the luxurious Median court education his sons actually received. Raised without discipline by women and eunuchs, the sons proved catastrophic rulers: one committed fratricide, and Cambyses lost the empire through his own drunken brutality. Only Darius and his seven fellow conspirators later restored Persian power.
distilled: Cyrus's sons, raised in Median luxury instead of Persian hardiness, destroyed through fratricide and madness what their father built -- until Darius restored the empire.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger; Cleinias confirms the tradition. -- The contrast between Persian ruggedness and Median luxury is a standard Greek trope (cf. Herodotus 1.71, 1.126). The narrative follows Herodotus 3.27-38 (Cambyses' madness), 3.61-79 (the conspiracy of the seven, including the 'false Smerdis' episode -- the 'Eunuch' or magus). The seven chiefs who restored Darius include figures like Otanes, who proposed democracy (Herodotus 3.80-82) -- the famous 'Constitutional Debate.' The phrase 'what people always become when they are brought up unreproved' states a universal law of moral psychology: character requires correction. This is the foundation of the Laws' educational program. Compare Republic 548a-550b on how oligarchic character develops through household corruption.

narrative:
"Let us note the rest of the story. Observe, that Darius was not the son of a king, and had not received a luxurious education. When he came to the throne, being one of the seven, he divided the country into seven portions, and of this arrangement there are some shadowy traces still remaining; he made laws upon the principle of introducing universal equality in the order of the state, and he embodied in his laws the settlement of the tribute which Cyrus promised -- thus creating a feeling of friendship and community among all the Persians, and attaching the people to him with money and gifts. Hence his armies cheerfully acquired for him countries as large as those which Cyrus had left behind him.

Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes; and he again was brought up in the royal and luxurious fashion. Might we not most justly say: 'O Darius, how came you to bring up Xerxes in the same way in which Cyrus brought up Cambyses, and not to see his fatal mistake?' For Xerxes, being the creation of the same education, met with much the same fortune as Cambyses; and from that time until now there has never been a really great king among the Persians, although they are all called Great.

And their degeneracy is not to be attributed to chance, as I maintain; the reason is rather the evil life which is generally led by the sons of very rich and royal persons; for never will boy or man, young or old, excel in virtue, who has been thus educated. And this, I say, is what the legislator has to consider, and what at the present moment has to be considered by us. Justly may you, O Lacedaemonians, be praised, in that you do not give special honour or a special education to wealth rather than to poverty, or to a royal rather than to a private station, where the divine and inspired lawgiver has not originally commanded them to be given. For no man ought to have pre-eminent honour in a state because he surpasses others in wealth, any more than because he is swift of foot or fair or strong, unless he have some virtue in him; nor even if he have virtue, unless he have this particular virtue of temperance."
refashioned:
"Let us follow the rest of the story. Note that Darius was not a king's son and had not been raised in luxury. When he took the throne as one of the seven conspirators, he divided the country into seven provinces -- an arrangement whose traces still faintly survive. He legislated on the principle of universal equality within the state and formalized the tribute system that Cyrus had promised, thereby generating friendship and community among the Persians and winning the people's loyalty through fair distribution. As a result, his armies willingly conquered territories as vast as those Cyrus had left behind.

But Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes, who was raised in the same royal luxury as Cambyses. We might justly exclaim: 'Darius, how could you raise Xerxes the same way Cyrus raised Cambyses, without learning from that fatal error?' Xerxes, product of the same corrupting education, met essentially the same fate. And from that day to this, there has never been a truly great king among the Persians, despite the fact that they all bear the title 'Great.'

This decline is not a matter of chance. The cause is the corrupting life that the sons of the very wealthy and powerful typically lead. No person -- young or old, boy or man -- will ever excel in virtue who has been raised this way. This is what the legislator must consider, and what we must consider now. The Spartans deserve praise precisely because they do not award special honors or special education based on wealth rather than poverty, or royal birth rather than common status, except where their divinely inspired lawgiver commanded it. No one should receive pre-eminent honor in a state simply for being wealthier than others -- any more than for being a faster runner, more handsome, or physically stronger -- unless they also possess virtue. And even virtue alone is insufficient without the specific virtue of temperance."
elegant:
Darius, not a king's son, ruled wisely: he divided the empire equally, established fair tribute, and won loyalty through justice. His armies willingly conquered as much as Cyrus had. But his son Xerxes, raised in luxury like Cambyses, met the same fate -- and since then Persia has produced no truly great king despite the title.

This is not chance but causation: wealth and royal privilege corrupt character. No one raised in unearned luxury achieves virtue. The Spartans rightly honor virtue rather than wealth or birth. No one deserves pre-eminent honor for riches, speed, beauty, or strength alone -- only for virtue, and specifically the virtue of temperance.
summary: The Athenian contrasts Darius -- a non-royal who governed wisely through equality and fair distribution -- with his son Xerxes, whose luxurious upbringing replicated Cyrus's mistake with Cambyses. Since Xerxes, no Persian king has been truly great. The Athenian draws the universal lesson: wealth and privilege corrupt character, and honor should be awarded only for virtue, especially temperance. He praises Sparta for following this principle.
distilled: Darius governed wisely because he was not raised in luxury, but his son Xerxes repeated Cambyses' downfall -- proving that unearned privilege inevitably corrupts, and only temperance deserves public honor.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of the Laws' most important passages on the relationship between education, wealth, and political virtue. Darius I's administrative reforms (the satrapy system, tribute regularization) are historically attested (Herodotus 3.89-97). The apostrophe to Darius ('how came you to raise Xerxes...') is a powerful rhetorical device. The claim that no Persian king since Xerxes has been 'really great' reflects Greek contempt for later Achaemenid rulers, though Artaxerxes I and II were competent administrators. The praise of Spartan egalitarianism is somewhat idealized -- Sparta had significant wealth inequality -- but the principle that honor should follow virtue rather than wealth is foundational to the Laws' political program.
